english_head: To whom do you ascribe the authorship of your legal arrangements, Strangers? To a god or to some man? To a god, Stranger, most rightfully to a god. We Cretans call Zeus our lawgiver; while in Lacedaemon, where our friend here has his home, I believe they claim Apollo as theirs. Is not that so, Megillus? Yes. Do you then, like Homer, say that

english_head: Minos used to go every ninth year to hold converse with his father Zeus, and that he was guided by his divine oracles in laying down the laws for your cities? So our people say. And they say also that his brother Rhadamanthys,—no doubt you have heard the name,—was exceedingly just. And certainly we Cretans

english_head: would maintain that he won this title owing to his righteous administration of justice in those days. Yes, his renown is indeed glorious and well befitting a son of Zeus. True, Stranger; and as one proceeds further one finds in the groves cypress-trees of wonderful height and beauty,

english_head: and meadows too, where we may rest ourselves and talk. You say well. Yes, indeed: and when we set eyes on them we shall say so still more emphatically. So let us be going, and good luck attend us. Amen! And tell me now, for what reason did your law ordain the common meals you have, and your gymnastic schools and military equipment? english_head: is not a level country, like Thessaly: consequently, whereas the Thessalians mostly go on horseback, we Cretans are runners, since this land of ours is rugged and more suitable for the practice of foot-running. Under these conditions we are obliged to have light armour for running and to avoid heavy equipment; so bows and arrows are adopted as suitable because of their lightness. Thus all these customs of ours are adapted for war,

english_head: and, in my opinion, this was the object which the lawgiver had in view when he ordained them all. Probably this was his reason also for instituting common meals: he saw how soldiers, all the time they are on campaign, are obliged by force of circumstances to mess in common, for the sake of their own security. And herein, as I think, he condemned the stupidity of the mass of men in failing to perceive that all are involved ceaselessly in a lifelong war against all States. If, then, these practice

If this, then, is the right attitude for a State to adopt towards a State, is the right attitude for village towards village different? By no means. It is the same, you say? Yes. english_head: What is your meaning, my admirable sir? It is just in this war, my friend, that the victory over self is of all victories the first and best while self-defeat is of all defeats at once the worst and the most shameful. For these phrases signify that a war against self exists within each of us. Now let us take the argument back in the reverse direction. The highest good, however, is neither war nor civil strife—which things we should pray rather to be saved from—but peace one with another and friendly feeling. english_head: that there are two kinds, the one being that which we all call civil, which is of all wars the most bitter, as we said just now, while the other kind, as I suppose we shall all agree, is that which we engage in when we quarrel with foreigners and aliens—a kind much milder than the former. Certainly. Come, then, which kind of warriors, fighting in which kind of war, did you praise so highly, while blaming others? Warriors, apparently, who fight in war abroad.

english_head: At any rate, in your poems you have said that you cannot abide men who dare not face the gory fray Tyrtaeus and smite the foe in close combat. Tyrtaeus Then we should proceed to say, It appears, O Tyrtaeus, that you are chiefly praising those who achieve distinction in foreign and external warfare. To this, I presume, he would agree, and say Yes?

english_head: Of course. Yet, brave though these men are, we still maintain that they are far surpassed in bravery by those who are conspicuously brave in the greatest of wars; and we also have a poet for witness,—Theognis (a citizen of Sicilian Megara), who says: In the day of grievous feud, O Cyrnus, the loyal warrior is worth his weight in silver and gold.Theognis 5.77-8 Bergk Such a man, in a war much more grievous, is, we say, ever so much better than the other—nearly as much better, in fact, as the unio

english_head: with courage is better than courage by itself alone. For a man would never prove himself a loyal and sound in civil war if devoid of goodness in its entirety; whereas in the war of which Tyrtaeus speaks there are vast numbers of mercenaries ready to die fighting with well-planted feet apart, of whom the majority, with but few exceptions, prove themselves reckless, unjust, violent, and pre-eminently foolish. What, then, is the conclusion to which our present discourse is tending, and what point i

But that goodness which Tyrtaeus specially praised,

english_head: fair though it be and fitly glorified by the poet, deserves nevertheless to be placed no higher than fourth in order and estimation. We are degrading our own lawgiver, Stranger, to a very low level! Nay, my good Sir, it is ourselves we are degrading, in so far as we imagine that it was with a special view to war that Lycurgus and Minos laid down all the legal usages here and in Lacedaemon. How, then, ought we to have stated the matter? In the way that is, as I think, true and proper

english_head: when talking of a divine hero. That is to say, we should state that he enacted laws with an eye not to some one fraction, and that the most paltry, of goodness, but to goodness as a whole, and that he devised the laws themselves according to classes, though not the classes which the present devisers propound. For everyone now brings forward and devises just the class which he needs: one man deals with inheritances and heiresses, another with cases of battery, and so on

english_head: in endless variety. But what we assert is that the devising of laws, when rightly conducted, follows the procedure which we have now commenced. Indeed, I greatly admire the way you opened your exposition of the laws; for to make a start with goodness and say that that was the aim of the lawgiver is the right way. But in your further statement that he legislated wholly with reference to a fraction of goodness, and that the smallest fraction, you seemed to me to be in error, and all this latter pa

greek_head: λέγειν αὐτός τε ἀκούειν βούλει σοι φράζω Πάνυ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: legw autos te akouw boulomai su frazw panu men oun eimi cenos xrh eipon e krhth nomos ou eimi math diaferontws en pas eudokimos o ellhn exw gar orqos o autos xraomai eudaimwn apotelew pas gar agaqos porizw diplazw de agaqos eimi o men anqrwpinos o de qeios artaw de ek o qew eteros kai ean men dexomai tis o megas polis
english_head: Shall I tell you? Yes, by all means. O Stranger (thus you ought to have said), it is not for nothing that the laws of the Cretans are held in superlatively high repute among all the Hellenes. For they are true laws inasmuch as they effect the well-being of those who use them by supplying all that are good. Now goods are of two kinds, human and divine; and the human goods are dependent on the divine, and he who receives the greater acquires also the less, or else he is bereft of both.

greek_head: κτᾶται καὶ τὰ ἐλάττονα εἰ δὲ μή στέρεται ἀμφοῖν ἔστι δὲ τὰ μὲν ἐλάττονα ὧν ἡγεῖται μὲν ὑγίεια κάλλος δὲ δεύτερον
greek_tokens: ktaomai kai o elasswn ei de mh steromai amfw eimi de o men elasswn os hgeomai men ugieia kallos de deuteros o de tritos isxus eis te dromos kai eis o allos passw kinhsis o swma tetartos de dh ploutos ou tuflos alla ocus blepw ean ama epomai fronhsis o dh proteros au o qew hgemonew eimi agaqos o fronhsis deuteros de meta neomai swfrwn yuxazw ecis ek de outos meta andreia kerannumi tritos an eaw dikaiosunh tetartos de
english_head: The lesser goods are those of which health ranks first, beauty second; the third is strength, in running and all other bodily exercises; and the fourth is wealth—no blind god Plutus, but keen of sight, provided that he has wisdom for companion. And wisdom, in turn, has first place among the goods that are divine, and rational temperance of soul comes second; from these two, when united with courage, there issues justice, as the third;

greek_head: ἀνδρεία ταῦτα δὲ πάντα ἐκείνων ἔμπροσθεν τέτακται φύσει καὶ δὴ καὶ τῷ νομοθέτῃ τακτέον οὕτως μετὰ δὲ ταῦτα
greek_tokens: andreia outos de pas ekeinos emprosqen tassw fusis kai dh kai o nomoqeths takteon outws meta de outos o allos prostacis o poliths eis outos blepw autos eimi diakeleusteon outos de o men anqrwpinos eis o qeios o de qeios eis o hgemwn new sumpas blepw peri te gamos allhlwn epikoinaomai meta te outos en o o pais gennhsis kai trofh osos
english_head: and the fourth is courage. Now all these are by nature ranked before the human goods, and verily the law-giver also must so rank them. Next, it must be proclaimed to the citizens that all the other instructions they receive have these in view; and that, of these goods themselves, the human look up to the divine, and the divine to reason as their chief. And in regard to their marriage connections, and to their subsequent breeding and rearing of children, male and female, both during youth and in

Moreover, in the matter of anger and of fear, and of all the disturbances which befall souls owing to misfortune, and of all the avoidances thereof which occur in good-fortune, and of all the experiences which confront men through disease or war or penury or their opposites,—

It is necessary, in the next place, for the law-giver to keep a watch on the methods employed by the citizens in gaining and spending money, and to supervise the associations they form with one another, and the dissolutions thereof, whether they be voluntary or under compulsion; he must observe the manner in which they conduct each of these mutual transactions, and note

Then finally, when he arrives at the completion of the whole constitution, he has to consider in what manner in each case the burial of the dead should be carried out, and what honors should be assigned to them. This being settled, the framer of the laws will hand over all his statutes to the charge of Wardens—guided some by wisdom, others by true opinion—to the end that Reason, having bound all into one single system, may declare them to be ancillary neither to wealth

english_head: In this manner, Strangers, I could have wished (and I wish it still) that you had fully explained how all these regulations are inherent in the reputed laws of Zeus and in those of the Pythian Apollo which were ordained by Minos and Lycurgus, and how their systematic arrangement is quite evident to him who, whether by art or practice, is an expert in law, although it is by no means obvious to the rest of us. What then, Stranger, should be the next step in our argument? We ought, as I think, to d

And as soon as we have completed our treatment of the first theme, we shall take that as our model and by a discussion of the rest on similar lines beguile the way; and at the end of our treatment of goodness in all its forms we shall make it clear, if God will, that the rules we discussed just now had goodness for their

english_head: A good suggestion! And begin with our friend here, the panegyrist of Zeus—try first to put him to the test. Try I will, and to test you too and myself; for the argument concerns us all alike. Tell me then: do we assert that the common meals and the gymnasia were devised by the lawgiver with a view to war? Yes. And is there a third institution of the kind, and a fourth? For probably one ought to employ this method of enumeration also in dealing with the subdivisions (or whatever we ought to call

english_head: The third thing he devised was hunting: so I and every Lacedaemonian would say. Let us attempt also to state what comes fourth,—and fifth too, if possible. The fourth also I may attempt to state: it is the training, widely prevalent amongst us, in hardy endurance of pain, by means both of manual contests and of robberies carried out every time at the risk of a sound drubbing; moreover, the Crypteia, as it is called, affords a wonderfully severe training

greek_head: πρὸς τὰς καρτερήσεις χειμώνων τε ἀνυποδησίαι καὶ ἀστρωσίαι καὶ ἄνευ θεραπόντων αὐτοῖς ἑαυτῶν διακονήσεις

english_head: in hardihood, as the men go bare-foot in winter and sleep without coverlets and have no attendants, but wait on themselves and rove through the whole countryside both by night and by day. Moreover in our games, we have severe tests of endurance, when men unclad do battle with the violence of the heat,—and there are other instances so numerous that the recital of them would be well-nigh endless. Splendid, O Stranger of Lacedaemon! But come now, as to courage, how shall we define it? Shall we defi

greek_head: καὶ λύπας διαμάχην μόνον ἢ καὶ πρὸς πόθους τε καὶ ἡδονὰς καί τινας δεινὰς θωπείας κολακικάς αἳ καὶ τῶν σεμνῶν
greek_tokens: kai luph diamaxh monos h kai pros poqos te kai hdona kai tis deinos qwpeia kolakikos o kai o semnon oiomai eimi o qumos poiew khrinos oimh men outws pros outos sumpas ei goun mimnhskw o emprosqen logos hsswn tis ode kai polis legw autos eautou kai anhr eimi gar eimi cenos knwsie kai panu ge
english_head: or as against desires also and pleasures, with their dangerous enticements and flatteries, which melt men’s hearts like wax—even men most reverenced in their own conceit. The latter definition is, I think, the right one: courage is battling against them all. Earlier in our discourse (if I am not mistaken) Clinias here used the expression self-inferior of a State or an individual: did you not do so, O Stranger of Cnosus? Most certainly.

greek_head: Νῦν οὖν πότερα λέγομεν τὸν τῶν λυπῶν ἥττω κακὸν ἢ καὶ τὸν τῶν ἡδονῶν
greek_tokens: nun oun poteros legw o o luph hsswn kakos h kai o o hdona mallon egw dokew o o hdona kai pas pou mallon legw o upo o hdona kratew outos o eponeidistos hsswn eautou proteros h o upo o luph
english_head: At present do we apply the term bad to the man who is inferior to pains, or to him also who is inferior to pleasures? To the man who is inferior to pleasures more than to the other, in my opinion. All of us, indeed, when we speak of a man who is shamefully self-inferior, mean one who is mastered by pleasures rather than one who is mastered by pains.

greek_head: Ὁ Διὸς οὖν δὴ καὶ ὁ Πυθικὸς νομοθέτης οὐ δήπου χωλὴν τὴν ἀνδρείαν νενομοθετήκατον πρὸς τἀριστερὰ μόνον

english_head: Then surely the lawgiver of Zeus and he of Apollo did not enact by law a lame kind of courage, able only to defend itself on the left and unable to resist attractions and allurements on the right, but rather one able to resist on both sides? On both sides, as I would maintain. Let us, then, mention once more the State institutions in both your countries which give men a taste of pleasures instead of shunning them,—just as they did not shun pains but plunged their citizens into the midst of them

greek_head: αὐτῶν ποῦ δὴ τοῦτ' ἔστιν ταὐτὸν περὶ τὰς ἡδονὰς συντεταγμένον ἐν τοῖς νόμοις λεγέσθω τί τοῦτ' ἐστὶν ὃ καὶ
greek_tokens: autos pou dh outos eimi tautos peri o hdona suntassw en o nomos legw tis outos eimi o kai apergazomai su omoios pros te alghdwn kai pros hdona o autos andreios nikaw te o deomai nikh kai oudamos hsswn polemios o eggus eautou kai xalepos outws men toinun eimi cenos kaqa pros o alghdwn exw nomos antitassw polus eipon ou an
english_head: or induced them by rewards, to master them. Where, pray, in your laws is the same policy adopted in regard to pleasures? Let us declare what regulation of yours there is which causes the same men to be courageous toward pains and pleasures alike, conquering where they ought to conquer and in no wise worsted by their nearest and most dangerous enemies. Although, Stranger, I was able to mention a number of laws that dealt with mastery over pains, in the case of pleasures I may not find it equally

english_head: but I might perhaps furnish some minor instances. Neither could I in like manner give myself clear examples from the Cretan laws. And no wonder, my most excellent friends. If then, in his desire to discover what is true and superlatively good, any one of us should find fault with any domestic law of his neighbors, let us take one another’s remarks in good part and without resentment.

english_head: You are right, Stranger: that is what we must do. Yes, for resentment would ill become men of our years. Ill indeed. Whether men are right or wrong in their censures of the Laconian polity and the Cretan—that is another story; anyhow, what is actually said by most men I, probably, am in a better position to state than either of you. For in your case (your laws being wisely framed) one of the best of your laws will be that which enjoins that none of the youth shall inquire which laws are wrong

A very sound observation, Stranger; and just like a diviner,

english_head: because of the natural attraction of pleasures, they will suffer the same fate as those who are worsted by fears: they will, that is to say, in another and still more shameful fashion be enslaved by those who are able to hold out amidst pleasures and those who are versed in the art of pleasure,—people who are sometimes wholly vicious: thus their condition of soul will be partly enslaved and partly free, and they will not deserve to be called, without qualification, free men and men of courage. english_head: that especially encourage the use of gymnasia. And whether one makes the observation in earnest or in jest, one certainly should not fail to observe that when male unites with female for procreation the pleasure experienced is held to be due to nature, but contrary to nature when male mates with male or female with female, and that those first guilty of such enormities were impelled by their slavery to pleasure. And we all accuse the Cretans of concocting the story about Ganymede.

english_head: Because it was the belief that they derived their laws from Zeus, they added on this story about Zeus in order that they might be following his example in enjoying this pleasure as well. Now with the story itself we have no more concern; but when men are investigating the subject of laws their investigation deals almost entirely with pleasures and pains, whether in States or in individuals. english_head: Do we call the man who possesses only nautical science, whether or not he suffers from sea-sickness, a good commander on a ship—or what? By no means good, if along with his skill he suffers in the way you say. And how about the army commander? Is a man fit for command, provided that he has military science, even though he be a coward and sea-sick with a kind of tipsy terror when danger comes? Certainly not. And suppose he has no military skill, besides being a coward? You are describing an utter

greek_head: Τί δ' ἐπαινέτην ἢ ψέκτην κοινωνίας ἡστινοσοῦν ᾗ πέφυκέν τε ἄρχων εἶναι μετ' ἐκείνου τε ὠφέλιμός ἐστιν
greek_tokens: tis de epaineths h yekths koinwnia ostis h fuw te arxw eimi meta ekeinos te wfelimos eimi o de mhte oraw eaw pote orqos autos eautou koinwnew meta arxw aei de anarxos h meta kakh arxw suneimi oiomai dh pote o toioutos qewros o toioutos koinwnia xrhstos tis yegw h epainew pws de an mhdepote ge eidon mhde suggignomai
english_head: Now take the case of any social institution whatsoever which naturally has a commander and which, under its commander, is beneficial; and suppose that someone, who had never seen the conduct of the institution under its commander, but seen it only when with no commander or bad commanders, were to commend the institution or censure it: do we imagine that either the praise or the blame of such an observer of such an institution is of any value? Certainly not, when the man has never seen nor shared

greek_head: ὀρθῶς γενομένῳ μηδενὶ τῶν τοιούτων κοινωνημάτων Ἔχε δή τῶν πολλῶν κοινωνιῶν συμπότας καὶ
greek_tokens: orqos gignomai mhdeis o toioutos koinwnhma exw dh o polus koinwnia sumpoths kai sumposion qew an eis tis sunousia eimi kai sfodra ge outos oun mhs orqos gignomai hdh tis pwpote qeaomai kai sfeis men apokrinw radios ws oudepwpote o parapan ou gar epixwrios su outos oude nomimos egw de entugxanw te polus kai pollaxou kai proseti passw ws epos eipon dierwtaw kai sxedon oloc
english_head: that was properly conducted. Now stay a moment! Shall we lay it down that, of the numerous kinds of social institutions, that of banqueters and banquetings forms one? Most certainly. Now has anyone ever yet beheld this institution rightly conducted? Both of you can easily make answer—Never yet at all, for with you this institution is neither customary nor legal; but I have come across many modes of banqueting in many places, and I have also inquired into nearly all of them, and I have scarcely s

greek_head: μὲν οὐδεμίαν ὀρθῶς γιγνομένην ἑώρακα οὐδὲ ἀκήκοα μόρια δ' εἴ που σμικρὰ καὶ ὀλίγα τὰ πολλὰ δὲ σύμπανθ' ὡς εἰπεῖν
greek_tokens: men oudeis orqos gignomai oraw oude akouw morion de ei pou mikros kai oligos o polus de sumpanti ws eipon diamartanw pws dh outos eimi cenos legw eipon eti safhs egw men gar osper eipon apeiria o toioutos oude entugxanw
english_head: a single one that was in all points rightly conducted; for if any were right at all, it was only in a few details, and most of them were almost entirely on the wrong lines. What do you mean by that, Stranger? Explain yourself more clearly; for since we are (as you observed) without any experience of such institutions,

greek_head: ἂν ἴσως εὐθύς γε γνοῖμεν τό τε ὀρθὸν καὶ μὴ γιγνόμενον ἐν αὐτοῖς
greek_tokens: an isos euqus ge gignwskw o te orqos kai mh gignomai en autos eoika legw alla egw frazw peiraw manqanw o men gar en passw te sunodos kai koinwnia pracis ostis ws orqos pantaxou ekastos arxw eimi manqanw pws gar ou kai meis legw nundi maxomai ws andreios deomai o arxw eimi pws de ou o meis andreios o deilh upo fobos hsswn qorubew
english_head: even if we did come across them, we would probably fail to see at once what was right in them and what wrong. That is very probable. Try, however, to learn from my description. This you understand—that in all gatherings and associations for any purpose whatsoever it is right that each group should always have a commander. Of course. Moreover, we have recently said that the commander of fighting men must be courageous. Of course. The courageous man is less perturbed by alarms than the coward.

greek_head: Καὶ τοῦτο οὕτως Εἰ δ' ἦν τις μηχανὴ μηδὲν τὸ παράπαν δεδιότα μηδὲ
greek_tokens: kai outos outws ei de eimi tis mhxanh mhdeis o parapan deidw mhde qorubew efisthmi stratopedon strathgos ara ou outos an pas tropos prassw sfodra men oun nun de ge ou stratopedon peri legw arxw en androw omilia exqra exqros meta polemos filos de en eirhnh pros filos koinwnew filofrwn orqos
english_head: That is true, too. Now if there had existed any device for putting an army in charge of a general who was absolutely impervious to fear or perturbation, should we not have made every effort to do so? Most certainly. But what we are discussing now is not the man who is to command an army in time of war, in meetings of foe with foe, but the man who is to command friends in friendly association with friends in time of peace. Quite so.

greek_head: Ἔστιν δέ γε ἡ τοιαύτη συνουσία εἴπερ ἔσται μετὰ μέθης οὐκ ἀθόρυβος ἦ γάρ
greek_tokens: eimi de ge o toioutos sunousia eiper eimi meta meqh ou aqorubos eimi gar pws gar alla oimh pas enantion oukoun proteros men kai outos arxw deomai tis meis ws oudeis ge pragma poteros oun aqorubos ei dunatos eaw o toioutos arxw ekporizw deomai pws gar ou kai meis peri ge sunousia ws eoika autos fronimos eimi deomai gignomai gar fulac o te uparxw
english_head: Such a gathering, if accompanied by drunkenness, is not free from disturbance, is it? Certainly not; quite the reverse, I imagine. So those people also need, in the first place, a commander? Undoubtedly—they above all. Should we, if possible, provide them with a commander who is imperturbable? Certainly. Naturally, also, he should be wise about social gatherings. For he has both to preserve

greek_head: φιλίας αὐτοῖς καὶ ἔτι πλείονος ἐπιμελητὴς ὅπως ἔσται διὰ τὴν τότε συνουσίαν
greek_tokens: filios autos kai eti pleiwn epimelhths opws eimi dia o tote sunousia alhqhs oukoun nhfw te kai sofos arxw mequw deomai kaqisthmi kai mh enantion mequw gar mequw kai neos arxw mh sofos ei mh kakos apergazomai tis megas polus xraomai an agaw tuxh pampolus men oun oukoun ei men gignomai ws dunatos orqos outos en o polis o sunousia memfomai tis epikalew
english_head: the friendliness which already exists among the company and to see that the present gathering promotes it still further. Very true. Then the commander we set over drunken men should be sober and wise, rather than the opposite? For a commander of drunkards who was himself drunken, young, and foolish would be very lucky if he escaped doing some serious mischief. Uncommonly lucky. Suppose, then, that a man were to find fault with such institutions in States where they are managed in the best possib

greek_head: αὐτῷ τῷ πράγματι τάχ' ἂν ὀρθῶς ἴσως μέμφοιτο εἰ δὲ ἁμαρτανόμενον ὡς οἷόν τε μάλιστα ἐπιτήδευμά τις ὁρῶν
greek_tokens: autos o pragma taxa an orqos isos memfomai ei de amartanw ws oios te malista epithdeuma tis oraw loidorew proteros men dhlos ws agnoew outos autos gignomai ou orqos eimi ostis pas outos o tropos fainw ponhros despoths te kai arxw nhfw xwris prassw h ou sunnoew outos ostis mequw kubernhths kai pas pas arxw
english_head: having an objection to the institution in itself, he might perhaps be right in doing so but if a man abuses an institution when he sees it managed in the worst way possible, it is plain that he is ignorant, first, of the fact that it is badly conducted, and secondly, that every institution will appear similarly bad when it is carried on without a sober ruler and commander. For surely you perceive

greek_head: ἀνατρέπει πάντα εἴτε πλοῖα εἴτε ἅρματα εἴτε στρατόπεδον εἴθ' ὅτι ποτ' εἴη τὸ κυβερνώμενον ὑπ' αὐτοῦ
greek_tokens: anatrepw pas eite ploion eite arma eite stratopedon eiqe ostis pote eaw o kubernaw upo autos pantapasi outos ge alhqhs erw eimi cenos epi ode de egw legos tis pote an gignomai outos orqos o peri o posis nomimos agaqos an draw egw oios o nundi legw ei strateuma orqos hgemonia tugxanw nikh polemos o epomai an gignomai ou mikros agaqos kai
english_head: that a sea-captain, and every commander of anything, if drunk, upsets everything, whether it be a ship or a chariot or an army or anything else that under his captaincy. What you say, Stranger, is perfectly true. In the next place, then, tell us this:—suppose this institution of drinking were rightly conducted, of what possible benefit would it be to us? Take the case of an army, which we mentioned just now: there, given a right leader, his men will win victory in war, which is no small benefit;

greek_head: τἆλλ' οὕτω συμποσίου δὲ ὀρθῶς παιδαγωγηθέντος τί μέγα ἰδιώταις ἢ τῇ πόλει γίγνοιτ' ἄν
greek_tokens: allos outws sumposion de orqos paidagwgew tis megas idiwths h o polis gignomai an tis de pais eis h kai xoros paidagwgew kata tropos eis tis megas o polis fhmi an gignomai h outos outws erwtaw eipon an ws eis men braxus tis o polis gignomai an ofelos ei de oloc erwtaw paideia o paideuw tis megas o polis oninhmi ou xalepos eipon ostis paideuw men eu gignomai an anhr agaqos gignomai
english_head: either to individuals or to a State from the right regulation of a wine-party? Well, what great gain should we say would accrue to the State from the right control of one single child or even of one band of children? To the question thus put to us we should reply that the State would benefit but little from one; if, however, you are putting a general question as to what solid advantage the State gains from the education of the educated, then it is quite simple to reply that well-educated men wil

greek_head: δὲ τοιοῦτοι τά τε ἄλλα πράττοιεν καλῶς ἔτι δὲ κἂν νικῷεν τοὺς πολεμίους μαχόμενοι παιδεία μὲν οὖν φέρει
greek_tokens: de toioutos o te allos prassw kalos eti de kan nikaw o polemios maxomai paideia men oun ferw kai nikh nikh de eniote apaideusia polus gar ubristos dia polemos nikh gignomai murw allos kakh dia ubris empiplhmi kai paideia men oudepwpote gignomai kadmeios nikh de anqrwpos polus dh toioutos gignomai te kai eimi dokew egw eimi filos o en o oinon koinos
english_head: besides acting nobly in other ways. Thus, while education brings also victory, victory sometimes brings lack of education for men have often grown more insolent because of victory in war, and through their insolence they have become filled with countless other vices; and whereas education has never yet proved to be Cadmeian, the victories which men win in war often have been, and will be, Cadmeian. You are implying, my friend, as it seems to us, that the convivial gathering,

greek_head: διατριβὴν ὡς εἰς παιδείας μεγάλην μοῖραν τείνουσαν λέγειν ἂν ὀρθῶς γίγνηται
greek_tokens: diatribh ws eis paideia megas moira teinw legw an orqos gignomai tis meis exw an oun o meta outos eipon ws eimi o nun erw alhqhs o men alhqhs eimi cenos diisxurizomai outos outws exw polus amfisbhtew qeaomai ei de oph egw fainw deomai legw oudeis fqonos epei ormaw ge o logos peri nomos kai politeia poiew o nun outos autos dh peirw o sos dokew peri o
english_head: when rightly conducted, is an important element in education. Assuredly. Could you then show us, in the next place, how this statement is true? The truth of my statement, which is disputed by many, it is for God to assert; but I am quite ready to give, if required, my own opinion, now that we have, in fact, embarked on a discussion of laws and constitutions. Well, it is precisely your opinion

greek_head: νῦν ἀμφισβητουμένων καταμαθεῖν Ἀλλὰ χρὴ ποιεῖν οὕτως ὑμᾶς τε ἐπὶ τὸ μαθεῖν καὶ
greek_tokens: nun amfisbhtew katamanqanw alla xrh poiew outws su te epi o manqanw kai egw epi o dhlow peiraw amos ge pws sunteinw o logos proteros de egw akouw o toiosde o polis apas egw ellhn upolambanw ws filologos su eimi kai polulogos lakedaimwn de kai krhth o men braxulogos o de polunoia mallon h polulogia askew
english_head: about the questions now in dispute that we are trying to learn. Thus, then, we must do,—you must brace yourself in the effort to learn the argument, and I to expound it as best I can. But, first of all, I have a preliminary observation to make: our city, Athens, is, in the general opinion of the Greeks, both fond of talk and full of talk, but Lacedaemon is scant of talk, while Crete is more witty than wordy;

greek_head: σκοπῶ δὴ μὴ δόξαν ὑμῖν παράσχωμαι περὶ σμικροῦ πολλὰ λέγειν μέθης πέρι σμικροῦ πράγματος παμμήκη λόγον ἀνακαθαιρόμενος
greek_tokens: skopaw dh mh doca su parexw peri mikros polus legw meqh peri mikros pragma pammhkhs logos anakaqairw o de o kata fus autos diorqwsis ou an dunamai aneu mousikh orqoths pote safhs oude ikanos en o logos apolambanw mousikh de aneu paideia o pas ou an au pote dunamai outos de pampolus eimi logos oraw oun tis poiew ei outos men eaw en o
english_head: so I am afraid of making you think that I am a great talker about a small matter, if I spin out a discourse of prodigious length about the small matter of drunkenness. But the fact is that the right ordering of this could never be treated adequately and clearly in our discourse apart from rightness in music, nor could music, apart from education as a whole; and these require lengthy discussions. Consider, then, what we are to do: suppose we leave these matters over for the present,

greek_head: παρόντι μετεκβαῖμεν δ' εἰς ἕτερόν τινα νόμων πέρι λόγον Ὦ ξένε Ἀθηναῖε οὐκ οἶσθ' ἴσως ὅτι τυγχάνει
greek_tokens: pareimi metekbainw de eis eteros tis nomos peri logos eimi cenos aqhnaios ou oistos isos ostis tugxanw egw o estios o polis eimi su procenos isos men oun kai pas o pais epeidan akouw ostis tis eimi polis procenos outos tis eunoia ek neos euqus enduw ekastos egw o procenos o polis ws deuteros eimi patris meta o eautou polis kai dh kai egw nun tautos outos eggignomai akouw gar o pais euqus
english_head: and take up some other legal topic instead. O Stranger of Athens, you are not, perhaps, aware that our family is, in fact, a proxenus of your State. It is probably true of all children that, when once they have been told that they are proxeni of a certain State, they conceive an affection for that State even from infancy, and each of them regards it as a second mother-land, next after his own country. That is precisely the feeling I now experience. For through hearing mere children crying out—

greek_head: εἴ τι μέμφοιντο ἢ καὶ ἐπαινοῖεν Λακεδαιμόνιοι Ἀθηναίους ὡς Ἡ πόλις ὑμῶν ὦ Μέγιλλε ἔφασαν ἡμᾶς οὐ καλῶς
greek_tokens: ei tis memfomai h kai epainew lakedaimonios aqhnaios ws o polis su eimi megillos fhmi egw ou kalos h kalos rezw outos dh akouw kai maxomai pros autos uper su aei pros o o polis eis yogos agw pas eunoia exw kai egw nun o te fwnh prosfilhs su o te upo polus legw ws osos aqhnaia eimi agaqos diaferontws eimi toioutos dokew alhqhs legw monos gar aneu anagkh autofuhs qeia moira
english_head: whenever they, being the Lacedaemonians, were blaming the Athenians for anything or praising them—Your State, Megillus, has done us a bad turn or a good one,—through hearing such remarks, I say, and constantly fighting your battles against those who were thus decrying your State, I acquired a deep affection for it; so that now not only do I delight in your accent, but I regard as absolutely true the common saying that good Athenians are always incomparably good, for they alone are good

greek_head: ἀληθῶς καὶ οὔτι πλαστῶς εἰσιν ἀγαθοί θαρρῶν δὴ ἐμοῦ γε ἕνεκα λέγοις ἂν τοσαῦτα ὁπόσα σοι φίλον
greek_tokens: alhqhs kai outi plastos eimi agaqos qarsos dh egw ge eneka legos an tosoutos oposos su filos kai meis eimi cenos kai o para egw logos aekwn te kai apodexomai qarsos oposos boulomai legos ode gar isos akouw ws epimenidhs gignomai anhr qeios os eimi egw oikeios erxomai de pro o persikos deka etos proteros para su kata o o qeaomai manteia qusia te quw
english_head: not by outward compulsion but by inner disposition. Thus, so far as I am concerned, you may speak without fear and say all you please. My story, too, Stranger, when you hear it, will show you that you may boldly say all you wish. You have probably heard how that inspired man Epimenides, who was a family connection of ours, was born in Crete; and how ten years before the Persian War, in obedience to the oracle of the god, he went to Athens and offered certain sacrifices which the god had ordained

greek_head: τινας ἃς ὁ θεὸς ἀνεῖλεν καὶ δὴ καὶ φοβουμένων τὸν Περσικὸν Ἀθηναίων στόλον εἶπεν ὅτι δέκα μὲν ἐτῶν οὐχ ἥξουσιν ὅταν
greek_tokens: tis os o qeos anairew kai dh kai fobew o persikos aqhnaia stolos eipon ostis deka men etai ou hkw otan de erxomai apallassw prassw oudeis os elpizw pasxw te h draw plew kakos tote oun cenow su e progonos egw kai eunoia ek tosos egw su kai
english_head: he made this prophecy—They will not come for ten years, and when they do come, they will return back again with all their hopes frustrated, and after suffering more woes than they inflict. Then our forefathers became guest-friends of yours, and ever since both my fathers and I myself

greek_head: οἱ ἡμέτεροι ἔχουσιν γονῆς Τὰ μὲν τοίνυν ὑμέτερα ἀκούειν ὡς ἔοικεν ἕτοιμ' ἂν
greek_tokens: e hmeteros exw gonaw o men toinun umeteros akouw ws eoika etoimos an eaw o de emos boulomai men etoimos dunamai de ou panu radia omws de peirateon proteros dh oun pros o logos orizw paideia tis pote eimi kai tis dunamis exis dia gar outos fhmi iteon eimi o proxeirizw en o nun logos ufh egw mexri an pros o qeos afikneomai panu men oun draw outos eiper sos ge hdus
english_head: have cherished an affection for Athens. Evidently, then, you are both ready to play your part as listeners. But as for my part, though the will is there, to compass the task is hard: still, I must try. In the first place, then, our argument requires that we should define education and describe its effects: that is the path on which our present discourse must proceed until it finally arrives at the god of Wine. By all means let us do so, since it is your wish.

greek_head: Λέγοντος τοίνυν ἐμοῦ τί ποτε χρὴ φάναι παιδείαν εἶναι σκέψασθε ἂν ἀρέσκῃ τὸ λεχθέν
greek_tokens: legw toinun egw tis pote xrh fainw paideia eimi skeptomai an areskos o legw legos an legos dh kai fhmi o otioun agaqos anhr mellw eimi outos autos ek pais euqus meleth dew paizw te kai spoudazw en o o pragma ekastos proshkw oios o mellw agaqos eimi gewrgos h tis oikodomos o men oikodomew tis o paideios
english_head: Then while I am stating how education ought to be defined, you must be considering whether you are satisfied with my statement. Proceed with your statement. I will. What I assert is that every man who is going to be good at any pursuit must practice that special pursuit from infancy, by using all the implements of his pursuit both in his play and in his work. For example, the man who is to make a good builder

greek_head: οἰκοδομημάτων παίζειν χρή τὸν δ' αὖ γεωργοῦντα καὶ ὄργανα ἑκατέρῳ σμικρά τῶν ἀληθινῶν μιμήματα παρασκευάζειν τὸν
greek_tokens: oikodomhma paizw xrh o de au gewrgew kai organon ekateros mikros o alhqinos mimhma paraskeuazw o trefw autos ekateros kai dh kai o maqhma osos anagkaion promanqanw promanqanw oios tektwn metrew h staqmaomai kai polemikos ippeuw paizw h tis o toioutos allos poiew kai peiraw dia o paidia ekeise trepw o hdona kai epiqumia o pais e afikneomai autos deomai telos exw kefalaios dh paideia
english_head: must play at building toy houses, and to make a good farmer he must play at tilling land; and those who are rearing them must provide each child with toy tools modelled on real ones. Besides this, they ought to have elementary instruction in all the necessary subjects,—the carpenter, for instance, being taught in play the use of rule and measure, the soldier taught riding or some similar accomplishment. So, by means of their games, we should endeavor to turn the tastes and desires of the childre

greek_head: λέγομεν τὴν ὀρθὴν τροφήν ἣ τοῦ παίζοντος τὴν ψυχὴν εἰς ἔρωτα μάλιστα ἄξει τούτου ὃ δεήσει γενόμενον ἄνδρ' αὐτὸν
greek_tokens: legw o orqos trofh o o paizw o yuxh eis erws malista agnumi outos o dehsis gignomai anhr autos teleios eimi o o pragma aretaw oraw oun ei mexri outos ge osper eipon su areskw o legw pws gar ou mh toinun mhde o legw eimi paideia aoristos gignomai nun gar oneidizw epainew su ekastos o trofh legw ws o men paideuw egw eimi tis
english_head: education, we say, consists in that right nurture which most strongly draws the soul of the child when at play to a love for that pursuit of which, when he becomes a man, he must possess a perfect mastery. Now consider, as I said before, whether, up to this point, you are satisfied with this statement of mine. Certainly we are. But we must not allow our description of education to remain indefinite. For at present, when censuring or commending a man’s upbringing, we describe one man

greek_head: τὸν δὲ ἀπαίδευτον ἐνίοτε εἴς τε καπηλείας καὶ ναυκληρίας καὶ ἄλλων τοιούτων μάλα πεπαιδευμένων σφόδρα ἀνθρώπων
greek_tokens: o de apaideutos eniote eis te kaphleia kai nauklhria kai allos toioutos mala paideuw sfodra anqrwpos ou gar outos hgeomai ws eoika eimi paideia o nun logos an eaw o de pros areth ek pais paideia poiew epiqumhths te kai erasths o poliths gignomai teleios arxw te kai arxw efisthmi meta dikh outos
english_head: as educated and another as uneducated, though the latter may often be uncommonly well educated in the trade of a pedlar or a skipper, or some other similar occupation. But we, naturally, in our present discourse are not taking the view that such things as these make up education: the education we speak of is training from childhood in goodness, which makes a man eagerly desirous of becoming a perfect citizen, understanding how both to rule and be ruled righteously. This is the special form of nu

greek_head: τὴν τροφὴν ἀφορισάμενος ὁ λόγος οὗτος ὡς ἐμοὶ φαίνεται νῦν βούλοιτ' ἂν μόνην παιδείαν προσαγορεύειν τὴν δὲ εἰς
greek_tokens: o trofh aforizw o logos outos ws egw fainw nun boulomai an monos paideia prosagoreuw o de eis xrhma teinw h tis pros isxus h kai pros allos tis sofia aneu neomai kai dikh banausos su eimi kai aneleuqeros kai ou acios o parapan paideia kalew egw dh mhdeis onoma diaferw eautou alla o nundi logos egw omologew menw ws e ge orqos paideuw sxedon agaqos gignomai kai deomai dh o paideia
english_head: to which, as I suppose, our present argument would confine the term education whereas an upbringing which aims only at money-making or physical strength, or even some mental accomplishment devoid of reason and justice, it would term vulgar and illiberal and utterly unworthy of the name education. Let us not, however, quarrel over a name, but let us abide by the statement we agreed upon just now, that those who are rightly educated become, as a rule, good,

greek_head: μηδαμοῦ ἀτιμάζειν ὡς πρῶτον τῶν καλλίστων τοῖς ἀρίστοις ἀνδράσιν παραγιγνόμενον καὶ εἴ ποτε ἐξέρχεται δυνατὸν δ'
greek_tokens: mhdamos atimazw ws proteros o kalos o ariston anhr paragignomai kai ei pote ecerxomai dunatos de eimi epanorqow outos aei drasteos dia bios pas kata dunamis orqos kai sugxwrew o legw kai meis pala ge sugxwrew ws agaqos men eimi o dunamai arxw eautou kakh de o mh legw orqos safhs eti toinun analambanw outos autos ostis
english_head: and that one should in no case disparage education, since it stands first among the finest gifts that are given to the best men; and if ever it errs from the right path, but can be put straight again, to this task every man, so long as he lives, must address himself with all his might. You are right, and we agree with what you say. Further, we agreed long ago that if men are capable of ruling themselves, they are good, but if incapable, bad. Quite true. Let us, then, re-state more clearly

greek_head: ποτὲ λέγομεν καί μοι δι' εἰκόνος ἀποδέξασθε ἐάν πως δυνατὸς ὑμῖν γένωμαι δηλῶσαι τὸ τοιοῦτον
greek_tokens: pote legw kai egw dia eikwn apodexomai ean pws dunatos su gignomai dhlow o toioutos legos monos oukoun enos men egw ekastos autos tiqhmi nai duo de ktaomai en eautou sumboulos enantios te kai afrwn os prosagoreuw hdona kai luph eimi outos pros de outos amfw au doca mellw os koinos men onoma elpis idios de fobos men o pro luph
english_head: what we meant by this. With your permission, I will make use of an illustration in the hope of explaining the matter. Go ahead. May we assume that each of us by himself is a single unit? Yes. And that each possesses within himself two antagonistic and foolish counsellors, whom we call by the names of pleasure and pain? That is so. And that, besides these two, each man possesses opinions about the future, which go by the general name of expectations; and of these, that which precedes pain bears t

greek_head: ἐλπίς θάρρος δὲ ἡ πρὸ τοῦ ἐναντίου ἐπὶ δὲ πᾶσι τούτοις λογισμὸς ὅτι ποτ' αὐτῶν ἄμεινον ἢ χεῖρον ὃς γενόμενος
greek_tokens: elpis qarsos de o pro o enantios epi de pas outos logismos ostis pote autos ameinwn h xeirwn os gignomai dogma polis koinos nomos eponomazw mogis men pws efepw legos meis o meta outos ws epomai kai en egw meis tautos outos paqos en peri dh outos dianoeomai outwsi qauma men ekastos egw hgeomai o zwion qew eite ws paignion ekeinos eite ws spoudazw tis sunisthmi ou gar dh outos
english_head: and in addition to all these there is calculation, pronouncing which of them is good, which bad; and calculation, when it has become the public decree of the State, is named law. I have some difficulty in keeping pace with you: assume, however, that I do so, and proceed. I am in exactly the same predicament. Let us conceive of the matter in this way. Let us suppose that each of us living creatures is an ingenious puppet of the gods, whether contrived by way of a toy of theirs or for some serious

greek_head: γε γιγνώσκομεν τόδε δὲ ἴσμεν ὅτι ταῦτα τὰ πάθη ἐν ἡμῖν οἷον νεῦρα ἢ σμήρινθοί τινες ἐνοῦσαι σπῶσίν τε ἡμᾶς καὶ
greek_tokens: ge gignwskw ode de oida ostis outos o paqh en egw oios neuron h smhrinqos tis eneimi epomai te egw kai allhlwn anqelkw enantios eimi epi enantios prassw e dh diaorizw areth kai kakia kew eis gar fhmi o logos dew o elcis sunepomai aei kai mhdamh apoleipw ekeinos anqelkw o allos neuron ekastos outos
english_head: but this we do know, that these inward affections of ours, like sinews or cords, drag us along and, being opposed to each other, pull one against the other to opposite actions; and herein lies the dividing line between goodness and badness. For, as our argument declares, there is one of these pulling forces which every man should always follow and nohow leave hold of, counteracting thereby the pull of the other sinews:

greek_head: δ' εἶναι τὴν τοῦ λογισμοῦ ἀγωγὴν χρυσῆν καὶ ἱεράν τῆς πόλεως κοινὸν νόμον ἐπικαλουμένην ἄλλας δὲ σκληρὰς καὶ
greek_tokens: de eimi o o logismos agwgh xruseos kai iera o polis koinos nomos epikalew allos de sklhros kai sidhreos o de malakos ate xruseos eimi o de allos pantodapos eidos omoios dew dh o kalos agwgeus o o nomos aei sullambanw ate gar o logismos kalew men eimi praos de kai ou biaios deomai uphreths autos o agwgh opws an en egw o xruseos genos nikaw o allos
english_head: it is the leading-string, golden and holy, of calculation, entitled the public law of the State; and whereas the other cords are hard and steely and of every possible shape and semblance, this one is flexible and uniform, since it is of gold. With that most excellent leading-string of the law we must needs co-operate always; for since calculation is excellent, but gentle rather than forceful, its leading-string needs helpers to ensure that the golden kind within us may vanquish the other kinds.

greek_head: γένη καὶ οὕτω δὴ περὶ θαυμάτων ὡς ὄντων ἡμῶν ὁ μῦθος ἀρετῆς σεσωμένος ἂν εἴη καὶ τὸ κρείττω ἑαυτοῦ καὶ ἥττω

english_head: In this way our story comparing ourselves to puppets will not fall flat, and the meaning of the terms self-superior and self-inferior will become somewhat more clear, and also how necessary it is for the individual man to grasp the true account of these inward pulling forces and to live in accordance therewith, and how necessary for the State (when it has received such an account either from a god or from a man who knows) to make this into a law for itself and be guided thereby in its intercours

greek_head: σαφέστερον ἡμῖν διηρθρωμένον ἂν εἴη ἐναργεστέρου δ' αὐτοῦ γενομένου καὶ παιδεία καὶ τἆλλα ἐπιτηδεύματα ἴσως
greek_tokens: safhs egw diarqrow an eaw enarghs de autos gignomai kai paideia kai allos epithdeuma isos eimi mallon katafanhs kai dh kai o peri o en o oinon diatribh o docazw men an eimi faulos peri mhkos polus logos perissos erw fainw de taxa an isos o mhkos ge autos ou apacios eu legw kai perainw oti an o ge nun diatribh acios gignomai
english_head: Thus both badness and goodness would be differentiated for us more clearly; and these having become more evident, probably education also and the other institutions will appear less obscure; and about the institution of the wine-party in particular it may very likely be shown that it is by no means, as might be thought, a paltry matter which it is absurd to discuss at great length but rather a matter which folly merits prolonged discussion. Quite right: let us go through with every topic that se

greek_head: Λέγε δή προσφέροντες τῷ θαύματι τούτῳ τὴν μέθην ποῖόν τί ποτε αὐτὸ ἀπεργαζόμεθα
greek_tokens: legos dh prosferw o qauma outos o meqh poios tis pote autos apergazomai pros tis de skopaw autos epanerwtaw oudeis pw pros ostis outos de oloc koinwnew outos poios tis sumpitnw gignomai eti de safhs o boulomai peiraw frazw erwtaw gar o toiosde ara sfodros o hdona kai luph kai qumos kai erws o o oinon posis epiteinw polus ge
english_head: Tell me now: if we give strong drink to this puppet of ours, what effect will it have on its character? In reference to what particular do you ask this question? To no particular, for the moment: I am putting the question in general terms—when this shares in that, what sort of thing does it become in consequence? I will try to convey my meaning still more clearly: what I ask is this—does the drinking of wine intensify pleasures and pains and passions and lusts? Yes, greatly.

greek_head: Τί δ' αὖ τὰς αἰσθήσεις καὶ μνήμας καὶ δόξας καὶ φρονήσεις πότερον ὡσαύτως σφοδροτέρας ἢ πάμπαν ἀπολείπει
greek_tokens: tis de au o aisqhsis kai mnhmh kai doca kai fronhsis poteros wsautws sfodros h pampan apoleipw outos autos an katakorhs tis o meqh gignomai nai pampan apoleipw oukoun eis tautos afikneomai o o yuxazw ecis o tote oste neos eimi pas tis meis hkistos dh tote an autos eautou gignomai egkraths
english_head: And how about sensations and recollections and opinions and thoughts? Does it make them likewise more intense? Or rather, do not these quit a man entirely if he becomes surfeited with drink? Yes, they quit him entirely. He then arrives at the same condition of soul as when he was a young child? He does. So at that moment he will have very little control of himself?

greek_head: Ἥκιστα Ἆρ' οὖν πονηρότατος φαμέν ὁ τοιοῦτος
greek_tokens: hkistos ara oun ponhros fhmi o toioutos polus ge ou monos ara ws eoika o gerwn dis pas gignomai an alla kai o mequskw ariston eipon eimi cenos outos dh o epithdeuma esqw ostis logos epixeirhsis peiqw egw ws xrh geuw kai mh feugw pas sqenos kata o dunatos eoika eimi su goun fhmi kai etoimos eimi nundi legw
english_head: Very little. And such a man is, we say, very bad? Very, indeed. It appears, then, that not the grey-beard only may be in his second childhood, but the drunkard as well. An admirable observation, Stranger. Is there any argument which will undertake to persuade us that this is a practice we ought to indulge in, instead of shunning it with all our might so far as we possibly can? It appears that there is: at any rate you assert this, and you were ready just now to argue it.

greek_head: Ἀληθῆ μέντοι μνημονεύεις καὶ νῦν γ' εἴμ' ἕτοιμος ἐπειδήπερ σφώ γε ἐθελήσειν προθύμως ἔφατον
greek_tokens: alhqhs mentoi mnhmoneuw kai nun ge eimi etoimos epei sfeis ge eqelw proqumos fhmi akouw pws de ou akouw kan ei mhdeis allos xaris alla o qaumasths te kai atopos ei deomai ekwn pote anqrwpos eis apas fauloths eautou emballw yuxazw legw eimi gar nai tis de swma eimi etairos eis ponhria leptoths te kai aisxos kai adunamia qaumazw an ei pote tis
english_head: You are right in your reminder, and I am still ready to do so, now that you and Megillus have both expressed your willingness to listen to me. Of course we shall listen, if only on account of the surprising paradox that, of his own free will, a man ought to plunge into the depths of depravity. Depravity of soul, you mean, do you not? Yes. And how about plunging into a bad state of body, such as leanness or ugliness or impotence? Should we be surprised if a man of his own free will ever

greek_head: ἑκὼν ἐπὶ τὸ τοιοῦτον ἀφικνεῖται Πῶς γὰρ οὔ
greek_tokens: ekwn epi o toioutos afikneomai pws gar ou tis oun o eis o iatreion autos badizw epi farmakoposia agnoew oiomai ostis meta oligos usteron kai epi polus hmeros exw toioutos o swma oios ei dia telos exw mellw zaw ou an dexomai h o epi o gumnasion kai ponos eimi ou oida ws asqenew eis o paraxrhma gignomai pas outos oida kai ostis o meta outos wfeleia eneka ekwn poreuw
english_head: got into such a state? Of course we should. Well then, do we suppose that persons who go of themselves to dispensaries to drink medicines are not aware that soon afterwards, and for many days to come, they will find themselves in a bodily condition such as would make life intolerable if it were to last for ever? And we know, do we not, that men who go to the gymnasia for hard training commence by becoming weaker? All this we know. We know also that they go there voluntarily for the sake of the s

greek_head: Κάλλιστα Οὐκοῦν χρὴ καὶ τῶν ἄλλων ἐπιτηδευμάτων πέρι
greek_tokens: kallista oukoun xrh kai o allos epithdeuma peri dianoeomai o autos tropos panu ge kai o peri o oinon ara diatribh wsautws dianohteon eiper en outos en outos orqos dianoeomai pws de ou an ara tis egw wfeleia exw fainw mhdeis o peri o swma elasswn o ge arxh o swmaskia nikaw o o men meta alghdwn eimi o de mh
english_head: Quite true. Should one not take the same view of the other institutions also? Certainly. Then one must also take the same view of the practice of wine-drinking, if one can rightly class it amongst the others. Of course one must. If then this practice should be shown to be quite as beneficial for us as bodily training, certainly at the outset it is superior to it, in so far as it is not, like bodily training, accompanied by pain.

greek_head: Ὀρθῶς λέγεις θαυμάζοιμι δ' ἂν εἴ τι δυναίμεθα τοιοῦτον ἐν αὐτῷ καταμαθεῖν
greek_tokens: orqos legw qaumazw de an ei tis dunamai toioutos en autos katamanqanw outos autos dh nun ws eoika egw hdh peirateon frazw kai egw legos duo fobos eidos sxedon enantios dunamai katanoew poios dh o toiosde fobew men pou o kakos prosdokaw gignomai nai fobew de ge pollakis doca hgeomai docazw kakos prassw h legw tis o mh kalh os
english_head: That is true; but I should be surprised if we succeeded in discovering in it any benefit. That is precisely the point which we must at once try to make plain. Tell me now: can we discern two kinds of fear, of which the one is nearly the opposite of the other? What kinds do you mean? These: when we expect evils to occur, we fear them. Yes. And often we fear reputation, when we think we shall gain a bad repute for doing or saying something base;

greek_head: δὴ καὶ καλοῦμεν τὸν φόβον ἡμεῖς γε οἶμαι δὲ καὶ πάντες αἰσχύνην
greek_tokens: dh kai kalew o fobos egw ge oimh de kai pas aisxunh tis de ou outos dh duo elegos fobos os o eteros enantios men o alghdwn kai o allos fobos enantios de eimi o pleistos kai megas hdona orqos legw ara oun ou kai nomoqeths kai pas e kai mikros ofelos outos o fobos en timaw megas sebomai kai kalh aidw o outos qarsos enantion anaideia te prosagoreuw
english_head: and this fear we (like everybody else, I imagine) call shame. Of course. These are the two fears I was meaning; and of these the second is opposed to pains and to all other objects of fear, and opposed also to the greatest and most numerous pleasures. Very true. Does not, then, the lawgiver, and every man who is worth anything, hold this kind of fear in the highest honor, and name it modesty; and to the confidence which is opposed to it does he not give the name immodesty, and pronounce it to be

greek_head: καὶ μέγιστον κακὸν ἰδίᾳ τε καὶ δημοσίᾳ πᾶσι νενόμικεν Ὀρθῶς λέγεις
greek_tokens: kai megas kakos idios te kai dhmosios pas nomizw orqos legw oukoun o su allos polus kai megas o fobos egw outos swzw kai o en o polemos nikh kai swthria eis pros eis oudeis outws sfodra egw apergazomai duo gar oun eimi o o nikh apergazomai qarsos men polemios filos de fobos aisxunh peri kakazw eimi outos afobos egw ara deomai gignomai kai foberos
english_head: both publicly and privately, a very great evil? Quite right. And does not this fear, besides saving us in many other important respects, prove more effective than anything else in ensuring for us victory in war and security? For victory is, in fact, ensured by two things, of which the one is confidence towards enemies, the other, fear of the shame of cowardice in the eyes of friends. That is so. Thus each one of us ought to become both fearless and fearful;

greek_head: ἕκαστον ὧν δ' ἑκάτερον ἕνεκα διῃρήμεθα Πάνυ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: ekastos os de ekateros eneka diairew panu men oun kai meis afobos ge ekastos boulomai poiew fobos polus tis eis fobos agw autos meta nomos toioutos apergazomai fainw tis de otan epixeirew tis foberos poiew meta dikh ara ou anaisxuntia sumballw autos kai prosgumnazw nikh deomai poiew diamaxomai eautou o hdona h o men deilia o en eautou prosmaxomai kai
english_head: and that for the several reasons we have now explained. Certainly. Moreover, when we desire to make a person fearless in respect of a number of fears, it is by drawing him, with the help of the law, into fear that we make him such. Apparently. And how about the opposite case, when we attempt with the aid of justice to make a man fearful? Is it not by pitting him against shamelessness and exercising him against it that we must make him victorious in the fight against his own pleasures? Or shall w

greek_head: νικῶντα αὐτὴν δεῖ τέλεον οὕτω γίγνεσθαι πρὸς ἀνδρείαν ἄπειρος δὲ δήπου καὶ ἀγύμναστος ὢν τῶν τοιούτων ἀγώνων
greek_tokens: nikaw autos deomai teleios outws gignomai pros andreia apeiros de dhpou kai agumnastos eimi o toioutos agwnos ostis oude an hmisus eautou gignomai pros areth swfrwn de ara teleios eimi mh polus hdona kai epiqumia protrepw anaisxuntew kai adikew diamaxomai kai nikaw meta logos kai ergnumi kai texnh en te paidih kai en spoudh alla apaqhs eimi pas o toioutos oukoun o ge eoika logos an exw
english_head: that a man can become perfect, and no one unversed and unpracticed in contests of this sort can attain even half the excellence of which he is capable,—in the case of temperance, on the other hand, a man may attain perfection without a stubborn fight against hordes of pleasures and lusts which entice towards shamelessness and wrong-doing, and without conquering them by the aid of speech and act and skill, alike in play and at work,—and, in fact, without undergoing any of these experiences? It wo

greek_head: Τί οὖν φόβου φάρμακον ἔσθ' ὅστις θεὸς ἔδωκεν ἀνθρώποις ὥστε ὁπόσῳ πλέον ἂν ἐθέλῃ τις πίνειν αὐτοῦ
greek_tokens: tis oun fobos farmakon esqw ostis qeos didwmi anqrwpos wste oposos pleos an eqelw tis pinw autos tosoutos mallon autos nomizw kaqa ekastos posis dustuxhs gignomai kai fobew o pareimi kai o mellw eautou
english_head: Well then: in the case of fear does there exist any specific, given by God to men, such that, the more a man likes to drink of it, the more,

greek_head: πάντα καὶ τελευτῶντα εἰς πᾶν δέος ἰέναι τὸν ἀνδρειότατον ἀνθρώπων ἐκκοιμηθέντα δὲ καὶ τοῦ πώματος ἀπαλλαγέντα
greek_tokens: pas kai teleutaw eis pas deos eimi o andreios anqrwpos ekkoimaomai de kai o pwma apallassw palin ekastote o autos gignomai kai tis toioutos fhmi an eimi cenos en anqrwpos gignomai pwma oudeis ei de oun gignomai poqen esqw ostis pros andreia eimi an nomoqeths xrhsimos oios o toiosde peri autos kai mala exw an autos dialegw ferw eimi nomoqeths eite krhs eiqe ostis nomoqetew proteros
english_head: at every draught, he fancies himself plunged in misfortune and finally, though he be the bravest of men, he arrives at a state of abject terror; whereas, when he has once got relieved of the potion and slept it off, he always becomes his normal self again? What potion of the kind can we mention, Stranger, as existing anywhere? There is none. Supposing, however, that there had been one, would it have been of any service to the lawgiver for promoting courage? For instance, we might quite well have

greek_head: μὲν τῶν πολιτῶν ἆρ' ἂν δέξαιο βάσανον δυνατὸς εἶναι λαμβάνειν ἀνδρείας τε πέρι καὶ δειλίας
greek_tokens: men o poliths ara an dexomai basanos dunatos eimi lambanw andreia te peri kai deilia fhmi pou pas an dhlos ostis tis de meta asfaleia kai aneu kindunos megas h meta o enantios kai outos meta o asfaleia sunomologew pas xraomai de an eis o fobos outos agw kai elegxos en o paqhma wste anagkazw afobos gignomai
english_head: or anyone else, would not your first desire be to have a test of courage and of cowardice which you might apply to your citizens? Obviously everyone of them would say Yes. And would you desire a test that was safe and free from serious risks, or the reverse? All will agree, also, that the test must be safe. And would you utilize the test by bringing men into these fears and proving them while thus affected, so as to compel them to become fearless; employing exhortations admonitions and rewards,—

greek_head: παρακελευόμενος καὶ νουθετῶν καὶ τιμῶν τὸν δὲ ἀτιμάζων ὅστις σοι μὴ πείθοιτο εἶναι τοιοῦτος οἷον σὺ
greek_tokens: parakeleuomai kai nouqetew kai timaw o de atimazw ostis su mh peiqw eimi toioutos oios su tassw en pas kai gumnazw men eu kai andreios azhmios apallassw an kakos de zhmia epitiqhmi h o parapan ou an xraomai mhdeis allos egkalew o pwma kai pws ou an xraomai eimi cenos gumnasia goun eimi filos para o nun qaumastos rastwnh an eaw kaqa enos kai kata oligos kai kaqa oposos
english_head: but degradation for all those that refused to conform wholly to the character you prescribed? And would you acquit without penalty everyone who had trained himself manfully and well, but impose a penalty on everyone who had done so badly? Or would you totally refuse to employ the potion as a test, although you have no objection to it on other grounds? Of course he would employ it, Stranger. At any rate, my friend, the training involved would be wonderfully simple, as compared with our present me

greek_head: τις ἀεὶ βούλοιτο καὶ εἴτε τις ἄρα μόνος ἐν ἐρημίᾳ τὸ τῆς αἰσχύνης ἐπίπροσθεν ποιούμενος πρὶν εὖ σχεῖν ἡγούμενος
greek_tokens: tis aei boulomai kai eite tis ara monos en erhmia o o aisxunh epiprosqen poiew prin eu exw hgeomai oraw mh dew outws pros o fobos gumnazw pwma monos anti murw pragma paraskeuazw orqos an tis prassw eite tis eautou pisteuw fusis kai meleth kalos paraskeuazw mhdeis oknew meta sumpoths pleiwn gumnazw epideiknumi o en o o pwma anagkh
english_head: or to groups ever so large. Suppose, then, that a man, actuated by a feeling of shame and loth to show himself in public before he was in the best of condition, should remain alone by himself while undergoing this training against fears and relying on the potion alone for his solitary equipment, instead of endless exercises,—he would be acting quite rightly: so too would he who, trusting in himself that by nature and practice he is already well equipped, should have no hesitation in training in

greek_head: διαφορᾷ δύναμιν ὑπερθέων καὶ κρατῶν ὥστε ὑπ' ἀσχημοσύνης μηδὲ ἓν σφάλλεσθαι μέγα μηδ' ἀλλοιοῦσθαι δι'
greek_tokens: diafora dunamis uperqeos kai kras wste upo asxhmosunh mhde eis sfallw megas mhde alloiow dia areth pros de o esxatos posis apallassw prin afikneomai o pas hssa fobew anqrwpos o pwma nai swfronew gar an eimi cenos kai o toioutos outws prassw
english_head: with the result that because of his excellence he neither commits any grave impropriety nor loses his head, and who, before they came to the last round, should quit the company, through fear of the defeat inflicted on all men by the wine-cup. Yes, Stranger, this man too would be acting temperately.

greek_head: Πάλιν δὴ πρὸς τὸν νομοθέτην λέγωμεν τάδε Εἶεν ὦ νομοθέτα τοῦ μὲν δὴ φόβου σχεδὸν οὔτε θεὸς ἔδωκεν
greek_tokens: palin dh pros o nomoqeths legw ode eien eimi nomoqeths o men dh fobos sxedon oute qeos didwmi anqrwpos toioutos farmakon oute autos mhxanaomai o gar gohs ou en qoinh legos o de afobia kai o lian qarsew kai akairos o mh xrh poteros eimi pwma h pws legw eimi fhmi pou o oinon frazw eimi kai enantion exis outos o nundi legw pinw o anqrwpos autos eautou poiew proteros ilaos euqus
english_head: Once more let us address the lawgiver and say: Be it so, O lawgiver, that for producing fear no such drug apparently has been given to men by God, nor have we devised such ourselves (for quacks I count not of our company); but does there exist a potion for inducing fearlessness and excessive and untimely confidence,—of what shall we say about this? Presumably, he will assert that there is one,—naming wine. And is not this exactly the opposite of the potion described just now? For, first, it make

greek_head: μᾶλλον ἢ πρότερον καὶ ὁπόσῳ ἂν πλέον αὐτοῦ γεύηται τοσούτῳ πλειόνων ἐλπίδων ἀγαθῶν πληροῦσθαι καὶ δυνάμεως

english_head: he becomes filled with high hopes and a sense of power, till finally, puffed up with conceit, he abounds in every kind of licence of speech and action and every kind of audacity, without a scruple as to what he says or what he does. Everyone, I imagine, would agree that this is so. Undoubtedly. Let us recall our previous statement that we must cultivate in our souls two things—namely,

english_head: the greatest possible confidence, and its opposite, the greatest possible fear. Which you called, I think, the marks of modesty. Your memory serves you well. Since courage and fearlessness ought to be practised amidst fears, we have to consider whether the opposite quality ought to be cultivated amidst conditions of the opposite kind. It certainly seems probable. It appears then that we ought to be placed amongst those conditions which naturally tend to make us exceptionally confident and audaci

english_head: and excessive audacity, and fearful of ever daring to say or suffer or do anything shameful. So it appears. And are not these the conditions in which we are of the character described,—anger, lust, insolence, ignorance, covetousness, and extravagance; and these also,—wealth, beauty, strength, and everything which intoxicates a man with pleasure and turns his head? And for the purpose, first, of providing a cheap and comparatively harmless test of these conditions, and, secondly, of affording pra

english_head: with its playful testing—provided that it is employed at all carefully? For consider: in the case of a man whose disposition is morose and savage (whence spring numberless iniquities), is it not more dangerous to test him by entering into money transactions with him, at one’s own personal risk, than by associating with him with the help of Dionysus and his festive insight?

greek_head: αὐτῶν σφαλερώτερον ἢ συγγενόμενον μετὰ τῆς τοῦ Διονύσου θεωρίας ἢ πρὸς τἀφροδίσια ἡττημένης τινὸς ψυχῆς

english_head: And when a man is a slave to the pleasures of sex, is it not a more dangerous test to entrust to him one’s own daughters and sons and wife, and thus imperil one’s own nearest and dearest, in order to discover the disposition of his soul? In fact, one might quote innumerable instances in a vain endeavor to show the full superiority of this playful method of inspection which is without either serious consequence or costly damage. Indeed, so far as that is concerned, neither the Cretans,

english_head: I imagine, nor any other people would dispute the fact that herein we have a fair test of man by man, and that for cheapness, security and speed it is superior to all other tests. That certainly is true. This then—the discovery of the natures and conditions of men’s souls—will prove one of the things most useful to that art whose task it is to treat them; and that art is (as I presume we say) the art of politics: is it not so? Undoubtedly.

english_head: In the next place, we probably ought to enquire, regarding this subject, whether the discerning of men’s natural dispositions is the only gain to be derived from the right use of wine-parties, or whether it entails benefits so great as to be worthy of serious consideration. What do we say about this? Our argument evidently tends to indicate that it does entail such benefits; so how and wherein it does so let us now hear,

What I state is this,—that in children the first childish sensations are pleasure and pain, and that it is in these first that goodness and badness come to the soul; but as to wisdom and settled true opinions, a man is lucky if they come to him even in old age and; he that is possessed of these blessings, and all tha

english_head: is indeed a perfect man. I term, then, the goodness that first comes to children education. When pleasure and love, and pain and hatred, spring up rightly in the souls of those who are unable as yet to grasp a rational account; and when, after grasping the rational account, they consent thereunto that they have been rightly trained in fitting practices:—this consent, viewed as a whole, is goodness, while the part of it that is rightly trained in respect of pleasures and pains, so as to hate what

english_head: up to the very end, and to love what ought to be loved, if you were to mark this part off in your definition and call it education, you would be giving it, in my opinion, its right name. You are quite right, Stranger, as it seems to us, both in what you said before and in what you say now about education. Very good. Now these forms of child-training, which consist in right discipline in pleasures and pains, grow slack and weakened to a great extent

english_head: in the course of men’s lives; so the gods, in pity for the human race thus born to misery, have ordained the feasts of thanksgiving as periods of respite from their troubles; and they have granted them as companions in their feasts the Muses and Apollo the master of music, and Dionysus, that they may at least set right again their modes of discipline by associating in their feasts with gods. We must consider, then, whether the account that is harped on nowadays is true to nature? What it says is

Now, whereas all other creatures are devoid of any perception of the various kinds of order and disorder in movement (which we term rhythm and harmony), to men the very gods, who were given, as we said, to be our fellows in the dance, have granted the pleasurable perception of rhythm and harmony, whereby they cause us to move

english_head: and lead our choirs, linking us one with another by means of songs and dances; and to the choir they have given its name from the cheer implanted therein. Shall we accept this account to begin with, and postulate that education owes its origin to Apollo and the Muses? Yes. Shall we assume that the uneducated man is without choir-training,

english_head: and the educated man fully choir-trained? Certainly. Choir-training, as a whole, embraces of course both dancing and song. Undoubtedly. So the well-educated man will be able both to sing and dance well. Evidently. Let us now consider what this last statement of ours implies. Which statement? Our words are,—he sings well and dances well:

How then, if a man takes the good for good and the bad for bad and treats them accordingly? Shall we regard such a man as better trained in choristry and music when he is always able both with gesture and voice to represent adequately that which he conceives to be good, though he feels neither delight in the good nor hatred of the bad,—or when, though not wholly able to represent hi

There is a vast difference between the two cases, Stranger, in point of education. If, then, we three understand what constitutes goodness in respect of dance and song, we also know who is and who is not rightly educated but without this knowledge we shall never be able to discern whether there exists any safeguard for education

english_head: or where it is to be found. Is not that so? It is. What we have next to track down, like hounds on the trail, is goodness of posture and tunes in relation to song and dance; if this eludes our pursuit, it will be in vain for us to discourse further concerning right education, whether of Greeks or of barbarians. Yes. Well then, however shall we define goodness of posture or of tune? Come, consider: when a manly soul is beset by troubles,

How could they be, when even their complexions differ in color? Well said, my friend. But in, fact, while postures and tunes do exist in music, which deals with rhythm and harmony, so that one can rightly speak of a tune or posture being rhythmical or harmonious, one cannot rightly apply the choir masters metaphor well-colored to tune and posture; but one can use this langua

english_head: and one is right in calling those of the brave man good, and those of the coward bad. To avoid a tediously long disquisition, let us sum up the whole matter by saying that the postures and tunes which attach to goodness of soul or body, or to some image thereof, are universally good, while those which attach to badness are exactly the reverse. Your pronouncement is correct, and we now formally endorse it. Another point:—do we all delight equally

english_head: in choral dancing, or far from equally? Very far indeed. Then what are we to suppose it is that misleads us? Is it the fact that we do not all regard as good the same things, or is it that, although they are the same, they are thought not to be the same? For surely no one will maintain that the choric performance of vice are better than those of virtue, or that he himself enjoys the postures of turpitude, while all others delight in music of the opposite kind. Most people, however, assert that t

Inasmuch as choric performances are representations of character, exhibited in actions and circumstances of every kind, in which, the several performers enact their parts by habit and imitative art, whenever the choric performances are congenial to them in point of diction, tune or other features (whether from natura

And when men are right in their natural tastes but wrong in those acquired by habituation, or right in the latter but wrong in the former, then by their expressions of praise they convey the opposite of their real sentiments;

english_head: for whereas they say of a performance that it is pleasant but bad, and feel ashamed to indulge in such bodily motions before men whose wisdom they respect, or to sing such songs (as though they seriously approved of them), they really take a delight in them in private. Very true. Does the man who delights in bad postures and tunes suffer any damage thereby, or do those who take pleasure in the opposite gain therefrom any benefit? Probably.

In such a case it is, to be sure, inevitable that the man thus delighted becomes assimilated to those habits, good or bad, in which he delights, even though he is ashamed to prai

english_head: Now where laws are, or will be in the future, rightly laid down regarding musical education and recreation, do we imagine that poets will be granted such licence that they may teach whatever form of rhythm or tune they best like themselves to the children of law-abiding citizens and the young men in the choirs, no matter what the result may be in the way of virtue or depravity? That would be unreasonable, most certainly.

english_head: But at present this licence is allowed in practically every State, with the exception of Egypt. How, then, does the law stand in Egypt? It is marvellous, even in the telling. It appears that long ago they determined on the rule of which we are now speaking, that the youth of a State should practise in their rehearsals postures and tunes that are good: these they prescribed in detail and posted up in the temples,

Say rather, worthy in the highest degree of a statesman and a legislator. Still, you would find in Egypt other things that are bad. This, however, is a true and noteworthy fact, that as regards music it has proved possible for the tunes which possess a natural correctness to be enacted by law and permanently consecrated. Hence, as I said, if one could by any means succeed in grasping no principle of correctness in tune, one might then with confidence reduce them to legal form and prescription, since the tendency of pleasure and pain to indulge constantly in fresh music has, after all, no very great power to corrupt choric forms that are consecrated, by merely scoffing at them as antiquated. english_head: Such would evidently be the case, judging from what you now say. May we confidently describe the correct method in music and play, in connection with choristry, in some such terms as this: we rejoice whenever we think we are prospering, and, conversely, whenever we rejoice we think we are prospering? Is not that so? Yes, that is so. Moreover, when in this state of joy we are unable to keep still. True.

english_head: Now while our young men are fitted for actually dancing themselves, we elders regard ourselves as suitably employed in looking on at them, and enjoying their sport and merrymaking, now that our former nimbleness is leaving us; and it is our yearning regret for this that causes us to propose such contests for those who can best arouse in us through recollection, the dormant emotions of youth. Very true. Thus we shall not dismiss as entirely groundless the opinion now

english_head: commonly expressed about merrymakers,—namely, that he who best succeeds in giving us joy and pleasure should be counted the most skilful and be awarded the prize. For, seeing that we give ourselves up on such occasions of recreation, surely the highest honor and the prize of victory, as I said just now, should be awarded to the performer who affords the greatest enjoyment to the greatest number. Is not this the right view,

english_head: and the right mode of action too, supposing it were carried out? Possibly. But, my dear sir, we must not decide this matter hastily; rather we must analyze it thoroughly and examine it in some such fashion as this: suppose a man were to organize a competition, without qualifying or limiting it to gymnastic, musical or equestrian sports; and suppose that he should assemble the whole population of the State and, proclaiming that this is purely a pleasure-contest in which anyone who chooses may com

The natural result would be that one man would, like Homer, show up a rhapsody, another a harp-song, one a tragedy and another a comedy; nor should we be surprised if someone were even to fancy

An absurd question; for who could possibly pretend to know the answer before he had himself actually heard each of the competitors? Very well, then; do you wish me to supply you with the answer to this absurd question? By all means. If the tiniest children are to be the judges, they will award the prize to the showman of puppe

english_head: Certainly they will. And older lads to the exhibitor of comedies; while the educated women and the young men, and the mass of the people in general, will award it to the shower of tragedies. Most probably. And we old men would very likely take most delight in listening to a rhapsode giving a fine recitation of the Iliad or the Odyssey or of a piece from Hesiod, and declare that he is easily the winner. Who then would rightly be the winner of the prize? That is the next question, is it not? Yes.

english_head: Evidently we three cannot avoid saying that those who are adjudged the winners by our own contemporaries would win rightly. For in our opinion epic poetry is by far the best to be found nowadays anywhere in any State in the world. Of course. Thus much I myself am willing to concede to the majority of men,—that the criterion of music should be pleasure not, however, the pleasure of any chance person; rather I should regard that music which pleases the best men

english_head: and the highly educated as about the best, and as quite the best if it pleases the one man who excels all others in virtue and education. And we say that the judges of these matters need virtue for the reason that they need to possess not only wisdom in general, but especially courage. For the true judge should not take his verdicts from the dictation of the audience, nor yield weakly to the uproar of the crowd or his own lack of education; nor again, when he knows the truth, should he give his

english_head: For, rightly speaking, the judge sits not as a pupil, but rather as a teacher of the spectators, being ready to oppose those who offer them pleasure in a way that is unseemly or wrong; and that is what the present law of Sicily and Italy actually does: by entrusting the decision to the spectators, who award the prize by show of hands, not only has it corrupted the poets
