english_head: is unseemly, but we shall forbid loud mourning and lamentation outside the house, and we shall prohibit the carrying out of the dead on to the open roads and making lamentation while he is borne through the streets, and the funeral party must be outside the city-bounds before day-break. These shall be the legal regulations regarding such matters: he that obeys them shall be free from penalty, but he that disobeys a single one of the Law-wardens shall be penalized by them all

english_head: with the penalty adjudged by all in common. All other interments of the dead, or disposal of corpses without interment in the cases of parricides, temple-robbers, and all such criminals,—have been previously dealt with and laid down by law, so that our task of legislation has nearly come to an end. But in every case, the full end does not consist in the doing, gaining or founding of an object; rather our view should be that it is only when we have discovered a means of salvation, endless and com

english_head: the whole of our creation is incomplete. You say well, Stranger; but explain to us yet more clearly the purport of your last observation. O Clinias, many of the sayings of old time have been nobly uttered, and of these not the least, I may say, are the titles given to the Fates. What titles, pray? That the first of them is Lachesis, the second Clotho, and Atropos the savior-third —she that bestows on the dooms ratified by Clotho the quality of irreversibility.

greek_head: ἀπεργαζομένων δύναμιν ἃ δὴ καὶ πόλει καὶ πολιτείᾳ δεῖ μὴ μόνον ὑγίειαν καὶ σωτηρίαν τοῖς σώμασι παρασκευάζειν

english_head: She it is that must furnish also to the State and its citizens, not merely health and salvation for their bodies, but also right legality in their souls, or rather the salvation of the laws. And this, as it seems clear to me, is what our laws still lack—namely, a right mode of naturally implanting in them this irreversible quality. The point you mention is a serious one, if it is really impossible to discover a means whereby everything may acquire some such quality.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' ἔστι μὴν δυνατόν ὥς γέ μοι τὰ νῦν παντάπασι καταφαίνεται

english_head: Nay, but it is possible, as I now perceive quite clearly. Then let us by no means desist until we have secured this very quality for the laws we have stated; for it would be ridiculous for us to have wasted all this labor on an object, and then not base it on any firm foundation. You are right in your exhortation, and you will find me as ready as yourself to proceed. Very good. Then what is it you say will prove a means of salvation to our polity and its laws, and how will it do so?

english_head: every member must bring with him one of the young men, not less than thirty years old, whom he has first selected as being both by nature and training a suitable person; after selecting him, he shall introduce him among the members, and if they also approve, he shall keep him as a colleague, but if they disapprove, the fact of his original selection must be concealed from all the rest, and especially from the person thus rejected. english_head: our previous discourse? It was. Resuming, then, the subject of this synod, I will say this:—If one were to lay this down as an anchor for the whole State, possessing all the requisite conditions,—then, I affirm, it would secure the salvation of all that we desire. How so? Now will be the time for us to display no lack of zeal in declaring truly what follows. Excellently spoken! Proceed as you propose.

english_head: It is probable. But what kind of reason is it which, when combined with senses, will afford salvation to ships in stormy weather and calm? On shipboard is it not the pilot and the sailors who, by combining the senses with the pilot reason, secure salvation both for themselves and for all that belongs to the ship? Of course. There is no need of many examples to illustrate this. Consider, for instance, what would be the right mark for a general to set up to shoot at in the case of an army, or the

Certainly. But if a doctor were ignorant of that bodily condition which we have now called health, or a general ignorant of victory, or any of the other matters we have mentioned, could he possibly be thought to possess reason about any of these things? So now, in our present case, if our settlement of the country is to be finally completed, there must, it would seem, exist in it some element which knows, in the first place, what that political aim, of which we are speaking, really is, and, secondly, in what manner it may attain this aim, and which of the laws, in the first instance, and secondly of men, gives it

english_head: if, being thus void of reason and void of sense, it acts at haphazard always in all its actions. Very true. In which, then, of the parts or institutions of our State have we now got anything so framed as to prove an adequate safeguard of this kind? Can we answer that question? No, Stranger; at least, not clearly. But if I must make a guess, it seems to me that this discourse of yours is leading up to that synod which has to meet at night, as you said just now.

english_head: An excellent reply, Clinias! And, as our present discourse shows, this synod must possess every virtue; and the prime virtue is not to keep shifting its aim among a number of objects, but to concentrate its gaze always on one particular mark, and at that one mark to shoot, as it were, all its arrows continually. Most certainly. So now we shall understand that it is by no means surprising if the legal customs in States keep shifting, seeing that different parts of the codes in each State look in

english_head: the aim of justice is to enable a certain class of people to rule in the State (whether they be really superior, or inferior), while with others the aim is how to acquire wealth (whether or not they be somebody’s slaves); and others again direct their efforts to winning a life of freedom. english_head: Then, Stranger, was not the view we stated long ago the right one? We said that all our laws must always aim at one single object, which, as we agreed, is quite rightly named virtue. Yes. And we stated that virtue consists of four things. Certainly. And that the chief of all the four is reason, at which the other three, as well as everything else, should aim. You follow us admirably, Clinias; and now follow us in what comes next. In the case of the pilot, the doctor, and the general, reason is d

english_head: as we said, towards the one object of aim which is proper in each case; and now we are at the point of examining reason in the case of a statesman, and, addressing it as a man, we shall question it thus:—O admirable sir, what is your aim? Medical reason is able to state clearly the one single object at which it aims; so will you be unable to state your one object—you who are superior, as perhaps you will say, to all the wise? Can you two, Megillus and Clinias, define that object on his behalf, a

Well then, can you declare that we need zeal in discerning both the object itself as a whole and the forms it assumes? Illustrate what you mean by the forms you speak of. For example, when we said that there are four forms of virtue, obviously, since there are four, we must assert that each is a separate one. Certainly. And yet we call them all by one name: we assert that courage is virtue, and wi

english_head: and the other two likewise, as though they were really not a plurality, but solely this one thing—virtue. Very true. Now it is not hard to explain wherein these two (and the rest) differ from one another, and how they have got two names; but to explain why we have given the one name virtue to both of them (and to the rest) is no longer an easy matter. How do you mean? It is not hard to make clear my meaning. Let one of us adopt the role of questioner, the other of answerer. In what way?

greek_head: Ἐρώτησόν με τί ποτε ἓν προσαγορεύοντες ἀρετὴν ἀμφότερα δύο πάλιν αὐτὰ προσείπομεν τὸ μὲν ἀνδρείαν
greek_tokens: erwtaw egw tis pote eis prosagoreuw areth amfoteros duo palin autos proseipon o men andreia o de fronhsis eraomai gar su o aitios ostis o men eimi peri fobos e kai o qhrion metexw o andreia kai o ge o pais hqos o panu neos aneu gar logos kai fusis gignomai andreia yuxh aneu de au logos yuxh fronimos te kai new exw oute gignomai pwpote oute eimi oude auqis pote gignomai ws eimi eteros alhqhs legw
english_head: Do you ask me this question—why, when calling both the two by the single name of virtue, did we again speak of them as two—courage and wisdom? Then I shall tell you the reason,—which is, that the one of them has to do with fear, namely courage, in which beasts also share, and the characters of very young children; for a courageous soul comes into existence naturally and without reasoning, but without reasoning there never yet came into existence, and there does not nor ever will exist, a soul th

greek_head: Ἧι μὲν τοίνυν ἐστὸν διαφόρω καὶ δύο σὺ παρ' ἐμοῦ ἀπείληφας τῷ λόγῳ ᾗ δὲ ἓν καὶ ταὐτόν σὺ πάλιν
greek_tokens: h men toinun eimi diaforos kai duo su para egw apolambanw o logos h de eis kai tautos su palin apodidwmi egw dianoeomai de ws era kai oph tessares eimi eis eimi kai egw de acios sew deiknumi ws eis palin oph tessares kai dh o meta outos skopaw o oida ikanos peri ostis os eimi men onoma eimi de au kai logos poteros monos efisthmi onoma xrewn o de logos agnoew h o ge eimi tis kai peri o diaferw
english_head: Wherein they differ and are two you have now learnt from my reply. So do you, in turn, inform me how it is that they are one and identical. Imagine you are also going to tell me how it is that, though four, they are yet one; and then, after you have shown me how they are one, do you again ask me how they are four. And after that, let us enquire regarding the person who has full knowledge of any objects which possess both a name and a definition, whether he ought to know the name only, and not kn

greek_head: μεγέθει τε καὶ κάλλει πάντα τὰ τοιαῦτα ἀγνοεῖν αἰσχρόν Ἔοικεν γοῦν
greek_tokens: megeqos te kai kallos pas o toioutos agnoew aisxros eoika goun megas dh tis nomoqeths te kai nomofulac kai os aretaw pas diaferw oiomai kai nikhthrios outos autos lambanw h outos autos peri os nun legw andreia swfrosunos dikaiosunh fronhsis kai pws outos dh peri o echghths o didaskalos o nomoqeths o allos o fulac o deomai
english_head: and importance. It would certainly seem to be so. For the lawgiver and the Law-warden, and for him who thinks he surpasses all men in virtue and who has won prizes for just such qualities, is there anything more important than these very qualities with which we are now dealing—courage, temperance, justice, and wisdom? Impossible. In regard to these matters, is it not right that the interpreters, the teachers, the lawgivers, as the wardens of the rest, in dealing with him that requires knowledge

greek_head: γνῶναί τε καὶ εἰδέναι ἢ τῷ δεομένῳ κολάζεσθαί τε καὶ ἐπιπλῆξαι ἁμαρτάνοντι πότερον οὐ διδάσκοντα ἣν δύναμιν ἔχει
greek_tokens: gignwskw te kai oida h o deomai kolazw te kai epiplhssw amartanw poteros ou didaskw os dunamis exis kakia te kai areth kai pantws dhlow diaferw o allos alla h poihths tis erxomai eis o polis h paideuths neos faskw eimi beltiwn fainw o pas areth nikaw eita en o toioutos polis opou mh logos ergon te ikanos fulac eien aretaw peri gignwskw ikanos qaumastos tis outos o polis afulaktos eimi pasxw
english_head: should excel all others in the art of instructing him in the quality of vice and virtue and exhibiting it fully? Or is some poet who comes into the State, or one who calls himself a trainer of youth, to be accounted evidently superior to him that has won prizes for all the virtues? In a State like that, where there are no wardens who are competent both in word and deed, and possessed of a competent knowledge of virtue,—is it surprising, I ask, if such a State, all unwarded as it is, suffers the

greek_head: ἃ πολλαὶ πάσχουσι τῶν νῦν πόλεων Οὐδέν γε ὡς εἰκός
greek_tokens: o polus pasxw o nun polis oudeis ge ws eoika tis oun o legw nun poihteos egw h pws o fulac akribhs o polus peri aretaw ergon kai logos kataskeuasteos h tis tropos o o emfrwn kefalh te kai aisqhsis omoiow egw o polis ws toioutos tis fulakh ktaomai en eautou pws oun dh kai tis tropos eimi cenos apeikazw autos toioutos tis legw
english_head: States which exist today? Not at all, I should say. Well then, must we do what we now propose, or what? Must we contrive how our wardens shall have a more accurate grasp of virtue, both in word and deed, than the majority of men? For otherwise, how shall our State resemble a wise man’s head and senses, on the ground that it possesses within itself a similar kind of wardenship? What is this resemblance we speak of and wherein does it consist?

greek_head: Δῆλον ὡς αὐτῆς μὲν τῆς πόλεως οὔσης τοῦ κύτους τῶν δὲ φυλάκων τοὺς μὲν νέους οἷον ἐν ἄκρᾳ κορυφῇ ἀπειλεγμένους
greek_tokens: dhlos ws autos men o polis eimi o kutos o de fulac o men neos oios en akra koruptw apolegw o eufuhs ocuths en paomai o yuxw exw peri oloc kuklos o polis oraw frourew de paradidwmi men o aisqhsis o mnhmh o presbus de ecaggelos gignomai pas o kata polis
english_head: Evidently we are comparing the State itself to the skull; and, of the wardens, the younger ones, who are selected as the most intelligent and nimble in every part of their souls, are set, as it were, like the eyes, in the top of the head, and survey the State all round; and as they watch, they pass on their perceptions to the organs of memory,—that is, they report to the elder wardens all that goes on in the State,—

greek_head: τοὺς δὲ νῷ ἀπῃκασμένους τῷ πολλὰ καὶ ἄξια λόγου διαφερόντως φρονεῖν τοὺς γέροντας βουλεύεσθαι καὶ ὑπηρέταις
greek_tokens: o de egw apeikazw o polus kai acios logos diaferontws fronew o gerwn bouleuw kai uphreths xraomai meta sumboulia o neos outws dh koinh swzw amfoteros ontws o polis oloc poteros outws legw h pws allos dew kataskeuazw mhs omoios pas ktaomai kai mh diakribow eimi os trefw te kai paideuw alla eimi qaumasios adunatos
english_head: while the old men, who are likened to the reason because of their eminent wisdom in many matters of importance, act as counsellors, and make use of the young men as ministers and colleagues also in their counsels, so that both these classes by their co-operation really effect the salvation of the whole State. Is this the way, or ought we to contrive some other? Should the State, do you think, have all its members equal instead of having some more highly trained and educated? Nay, my good sir, th

greek_head: Ἰτέον ἄρα ἐπί τινα ἀκριβεστέραν παιδείαν τῆς ἔμπροσθεν
greek_tokens: iteon ara epi tis akribhs paideia o emprosqen isos ara oun eis dh nun sxedon efaptw tugxanw an eimi eis xreia exw outos pantapasi men oun oukoun legw o ge pros ekastos akron dhmiourgos te kai fulac mh monos dew pros o polus blepw dunatos eimi pros de o eis epeigw gignwskw te kai gignwskw pros ekeinos suntassw pas sunoraw orqos
english_head: I suppose so. Will the type which we hinted at just now prove to be that which we require? Certainly. Did we not say that he who is a first-class craftsman or warden, in any department, must not only be able to pay regard to the many, but must be able also to press towards the One so as to discern it and, on discerning it, to survey and organize all the rest with a single eye to it? Quite right.

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν ἀκριβεστέρα σκέψις θέα τε ἂν περὶ ὁτουοῦν ὁτῳοῦν γίγνοιτο ἢ τὸ πρὸς μίαν ἰδέαν ἐκ τῶν πολλῶν καὶ
greek_tokens: ara oun akribhs skeyis qea te an peri ostis ostis gignomai h o pros eis idea ek o polus kai anomoios dunatos eimi blepw isos ou isos alla ontws eimi daimonios outos ou eimi safhs meqodos anqrwpos oudeis sos pisteuw eimi cenos sugxwrew dh kai outos poreuw legw anagkasteos ara ws eoika kai o o qeia politeia egw fulac akribhs eidon proteros tis pote dia
english_head: Can any man get an accurate vision and view of any object better than by being able to look from the many and dissimilar to the one unifying form? Probably not. It is certain, my friend, rather than probable, that no man can possibly have a clearer method than this. I believe you, Stranger, and I assent; so let us employ this method in our subsequent discourse. Naturally we must compel the wardens also of our divine polity to observe accurately, in the first place, what that identical element is

greek_head: πάντων τῶν τεττάρων ταὐτὸν τυγχάνει ὃ δή φαμεν ἔν τε ἀνδρείᾳ καὶ σωφροσύνῃ καὶ δικαιοσύνῃ καὶ ἐν φρονήσει ἓν
greek_tokens: pas o tessares tautos tugxanw o dh fhmi en te andreia kai swfrosunos kai dikaiosunh kai en fronhsis eis eimi areth eis dikaios an onoma prosagoreuw outos eimi filos ei men boulomai o nun oionper sfodra piezw mh anazw prin an ikanos eipon tis pote eimi eis o blepteon eite ws eis eite ws oloc eite amfoteros eite opws pote fuw h outos diafeugw egw oiomai pote
english_head: and which,—since it exists as a unity in courage, temperance, justice and wisdom,— may justly be called, as we assert, by the single name of virtue. This element, my friends, we must now (if we please) hold very tight, and not let go until we have adequately explained the essential nature of the object to be aimed at—whether, that is, it exists by nature as a unity, or as a whole, or as both, or in some other way. Else, if this eludes us, can we possibly suppose that we shall adequately grasp th

greek_head: ἡμῖν ἱκανῶς ἕξειν τὰ πρὸς ἀρετήν περὶ ἧς οὔτ' εἰ πολλά ἐστ' οὔτ' εἰ τέτταρα οὔθ' ὡς ἓν δυνατοὶ φράζειν ἐσόμεθα
greek_tokens: egw ikanos exw o pros areth peri eis oute ei polus eimi oute ei tessares oute ws eis dunatos frazw eimi oukoun ean ge egw sumboulos peiqw allos de pws mhxanaomai en o polis eggignomai outos egw ei de ara o parapan dokew eaw oraw dh xrewn hkistos nh o cenios eimi cenos qeos eateos pou o toioutos epei dokew egw orqos legw alla dh pws tis outos an mhxanaomai
english_head: Accordingly, if we follow our own counsel, we shall contrive somehow, by hook or by crook, that this knowledge shall exist in our State. Should we decide, however, to pass it over entirely—pass it over we must. Nay, Stranger, in the name of the Stranger’s God, we must by no means pass over a matter such as this, since what you say seems to us most true. But how is this to be contrived?

greek_head: Μήπω τὸ πῶς ἂν μηχανησαίμεθα λέγωμεν εἰ δεῖ δὲ ἢ μή πρῶτον βεβαιωσώμεθα τῇ συνομολογίᾳ πρὸς ἡμᾶς
greek_tokens: mhpw o pws an mhxanaomai legw ei deomai de h mh proteros bebaiow o sunomologia pros egw autos alla meis deomai ge eiper dunatos tis de dh peri kalew te kai agaqos tautos outos dianoeomai ws polus eimi monos ekastos outos o fulac egw gnwsteos h kai opws eis te kai oph sxedon eoika ek anagkh dew kai opws eis dianoeomai
english_head: It is too early to explain how we are to contrive it: let us first make sure that we agree among ourselves as to whether or not we ought to do so. Well, surely we ought, if we can. Very well then; do we hold the same view about the fair and the good? Ought our wardens to know only that each of these is a plurality, or ought they also to know how and wherein they are each a unity? It is fairly obvious that they must necessarily also discern how these are a unity.

greek_head: Τί δ' ἐννοεῖν μέν τὴν δὲ ἔνδειξιν τῷ λόγῳ ἀδυνατεῖν ἐνδείκνυσθαι
greek_tokens: tis de ennoew men o de endeicis o logos adunatew endeiknumi kai pws andrapodon gar tis su legw ecis tis de peri pas o spoudaios ara egw autos logos ostis deomai o ontws fulac eimi o nomos ontws oida o peri o alhqeia autos kai logos te ikanos ermhneuw eimi kai o ergon sunakolouqew krinw o te kalos gignomai kai o mh kata fus pws gar ou
english_head: Well then, ought they to discern it, but be unable to give a verbal demonstration of it? Impossible! The state of mind you describe is that of a slave. Well then, do we hold the same view about all forms of goodness, that those who are to be real wardens of the laws must really know the true nature of them, and be capable both of expounding it in word and conforming to it in deed, passing judgment on fair actions and foul according to their real character? Certainly.

greek_head: Μῶν οὖν οὐχ ἓν τῶν καλλίστων ἐστὶν τὸ περὶ τοὺς θεούς ὃ δὴ σπουδῇ διεπερανάμεθα ὡς εἰσίν τε καὶ ὅσης
greek_tokens: mhs oun ou eis o kalos eimi o peri o qeos o dh spoudazw diaperainw ws eimi te kai osos fainw kurios dunamis oida te eis osos dunatos eimi outos anqrwpos gignwskw kai o men pleistos o kata polis suggignwskw o fhmh monos o nomos sunakolouqew o de fulakeus metexw mhde epitrepw os an mh diaponew o pas pistis lambanw o eimi peri qea o de mh epitroph eimi o mhdepote
english_head: And is not one of the fairest things the doctrine about the gods, which we expounded earnestly,—to know both that they exist, and what power they manifestly possess, so far as a man is capable of learning these matters; so that while one should pardon the mass of the citizens if they merely follow the letter of the law, one must exclude from office those who are eligible for wardenship, unless they labor to grasp all the proofs there are about the existence of gods? Such exclusion from office

greek_head: τῶν νομοφυλάκων αἱρεῖσθαι τὸν μὴ θεῖον καὶ διαπεπονηκότα πρὸς αὐτά μηδ' αὖ τῶν πρὸς ἀρετὴν ἔγκριτον
greek_tokens: o nomofulac airew o mh qew kai diaponew pros autos mhde au o pros areth egkritos gignomai dikaios goun ws legw o peri o toioutos argos h adunatos apokrinw prosw o kalh ara oun oida ostis duh eimi o peri qea agw eis pistis osos dierxomai en o prosqen poios eis men o peri o yuxh legw ws presbus
english_head: consists in refusing ever to choose as a Law-warden, or to number among those approved for excellence, a man who is not divine himself, nor has spent any labor over things divine. It is certainly just, as you say, that the man who is idle or incapable in respect of this subject should be strictly debarred from the ranks of the noble. Are we assured, then, that there are two causes, amongst those we previously discussed, which lead to faith in the gods? What two? One is our dogma about the soul,—

greek_head: τε καὶ θειότατόν ἐστιν πάντων ὧν κίνησις γένεσιν παραλαβοῦσα ἀέναον οὐσίαν ἐπόρισεν ἓν δὲ τὸ περὶ τὴν
greek_tokens: te kai qeios eimi pas os kinhsis genesis paralambanw aenaos ousia porizw eis de o peri o fora ws exis tacis astron te kai osos allos egkraths noos eimi o pas diakosmew o gar eidon outos mh faulos mhde idiwtikos oudeis outws aqeos anqrwpos pote fuw os ou enantion pasxw h o prosdokaw upo
english_head: and divine of all the things whose motion, when developed into becoming, provides an ever-flowing fount of being; and the other is our dogma concerning the ordering of the motion of the stars and all the other bodies under the control of reason, which has made a cosmos of the All. For no man that views these objects in no careless or amateurish way has ever proved so godless as not to be affected by them in a way just the opposite of that which most people expect.

greek_head: τῶν πολλῶν οἱ μὲν γὰρ διανοοῦνται τοὺς τὰ τοιαῦτα μεταχειρισαμένους ἀστρονομίᾳ τε καὶ ταῖς μετὰ ταύτης ἀναγκαίαις
greek_tokens: o polus e men gar dianoeomai o o toioutos metaxeirizomai astronomia te kai o meta outos anagkh allos texnh aqeos gignomai kaqoraw ws oios te gignomai anagkh pragma alla ou dianoia boulhsis agaqos peri tellw o de dh pws exw an eaw pas osper eipon enantion exis nun te kai oste ayuxos autos e dianoeomai dianoeomai qauma men oun
english_head: For they imagine that those who study these objects in astronomy and the other necessary allied arts become atheists through observing, as they suppose, that all things come into being by necessary forces and not by the mental energy of the will aiming at the fulfillment of good. What in fact is the real state of the case? The position at present is, as I said, exactly the opposite of what it was when those who considered these objects considered them to be soulless. Yet even then they were obje

greek_head: καὶ τότε ὑπεδύετο περὶ αὐτά καὶ ὑπωπτεύετο τὸ νῦν ὄντως δεδογμένον ὅσοι τῆς ἀκριβείας αὐτῶν ἥπτοντο ὅπως μήποτ'
greek_tokens: kai tote upoduomai peri autos kai upopteuw o nun ontws dokew osos o akribeia autos aptw opws mhpote an ayuxos eimi outws eis akribeia qaumastos logismos an xraomai new mh ktaomai kai tis tolmaw outos ge autos parakinduneuw kai tote legw ws noos eaw o diakosmew panth osos kata ouranos e de autos palin amartanw yuxazw fusis ostis presbus eaw swma
english_head: which is now actually held was suspected by all who studied them accurately—namely, that if they were soulless, and consequently devoid of reason, they could never have employed with such precision calculations so marvellous; and even in those days there were some who dared to hazard the statement that reason is the orderer of all that is in the heavens. But the same thinkers, through mistaking the nature of the soul and conceiving her to be posterior, instead of prior, to body,

greek_head: διανοηθέντες δὲ ὡς νεώτερον ἅπανθ' ὡς εἰπεῖν ἔπος ἀνέτρεψαν πάλιν ἑαυτοὺς δὲ πολὺ μᾶλλον τὸ γὰρ δὴ πρὸ τῶν
greek_tokens: dianoeomai de ws neos apas ws eipon epos anatrepw palin eautou de polus mallon o gar dh pro o omma pas autos fainw o kata ouranos ferw mestos eimi liqos kai gaia kai polus allos ayuxos swma dianemw o aitios pas o kosmos outos eimi o tote ecergazomai polus aqeoths kai dusxereia o toioutos aptw kai dh kai loidorhsis ge eperxomai poihths o filosofew kuwn mataios apeikazw
english_head: upset again (so to say) the whole universe, and most of all themselves; for as regards the visible objects of sight, all that moves in the heavens appeared to them to be full of stones, earth and many other soulless bodies which dispense the causes of the whole cosmos. These were the views which, at that time, caused these thinkers to incur many charges of atheism and much odium, and which also incited the poets to abuse them by likening philosophers to dogs howling at the moon, with other such

greek_head: χρωμέναισιν ὑλακαῖς ἄλλα τε αὖ ἀνόητ' εἰπεῖν νῦν δέ ὅπερ εἴρηται πᾶν τοὐναντίον ἔχει
greek_tokens: xraomai ulakh allos te au anohtos eipon nun de osper eromai pas enantion exis pws ou eimi pote gignomai bebaios qeosebhs qnhtos anqrwpos oudeis os an mh o legw outos nun duo lambanw yuxh te ws eimi presbus apas osos gonaw metalambanw aqanatos te arxw te dh swma pas epi de outos dh o nun erw pollakis o te erw
english_head: But today, as we have said, the position is quite the reverse. How so? It is impossible for any mortal man to become permanently god-fearing if he does not grasp the two truths now stated,—namely, how that the soul is oldest of all things that partake of generation, and is immortal, and rules over all bodies,—and in addition to this, he must also grasp that reason which, as we have often affirmed, controls what exists among the stars, together with the

greek_head: ἐν τοῖς ἄστροις νοῦν τῶν ὄντων τά τε πρὸ τούτων ἀναγκαῖα μαθήματα λάβῃ τά τε κατὰ τὴν μοῦσαν τούτοις τῆς κοινωνίας
greek_tokens: en o astron new o eimi o te pro outos anagkaion maqhma lambanw o te kata o mousa outos o koinwnia sunqeaomai xraw pros o o hqos epithdeuma kai nomimos sunarmottontws osos te logos exis outos dunatos
english_head: necessary preliminary sciences; and he must observe also the connection therewith of musical theory, and apply it harmoniously to the institutions and rules of ethics; and he must be able to give a rational explanation of all that admits of rational explanation.

greek_head: ᾖ δοῦναι τὸν λόγον ὁ δὲ μὴ ταῦθ' οἷός τ' ὢν πρὸς ταῖς δημοσίαις ἀρεταῖς κεκτῆσθαι σχεδὸν ἄρχων μὲν οὐκ ἄν
greek_tokens: eimi didwmi o logos o de mh outos oios su eimi pros o dhmosios areth ktaomai sxedon arxw men ou an pote gignomai ikanos oloc polis uphreths de an allos arxw oraw dh xrewn nun eimi kleinia kai megillos hdh pros o erw nomos apas osos dierxomai ei kai outos prosferw ws fulakh eimi kata nomos xaris swthria o o arxw nukterinos sullogos
english_head: He that is unable to master these sciences, in addition to the popular virtues, will never make a competent magistrate of the whole State, but only a minister to other magistrates. And now, O Megillus and Clinias, it is time at last to consider whether, in addition to all the previous laws which we have stated, we shall add this also—that the nocturnal synod of magistrates shall be legally established, and shall participate in all the education we have described, to keep ward over the State, and

greek_head: παιδείας ὁπόσης διεληλύθαμεν κοινωνὸν γενόμενον ἢ πῶς ποιῶμεν
greek_tokens: paideia oposos dierxomai koinwnos gignomai h pws poiew alla eimi lwstos pws ou prosferw an ph kai kata braxus dunamai kai meis pros ge o toioutos amillaomai pas sullhptwr gar outos ge su kai egw gignomai an proqumos pros de egw kai eteros isos euriskw dia o peri o toioutos empeiria te kai skeyis gignomai egw kai mala suxnos alla eimi cenos pas men mallon outos poreuteos hper kai o qeos egw sxedon agos tis de o tropos egw
english_head: or what are we to do? Of course we shall add this law, my excellent sir, if we can possibly do so, even to a small extent. Then, verily, let us all strive to do so. And herein you will find me a most willing helper, owing to my very long experience and study of this subject; and perhaps I shall discover other helpers also besides myself. Well, Stranger, we most certainly must proceed on that path along which God too, it would seem, is conducting us. But what is the right method for us to employ,

greek_head: γιγνόμενος ὀρθῶς γίγνοιτ' ἄν τοῦτο δὴ τὰ νῦν λέγωμέν τε καὶ ἐρευνῶμεν
greek_tokens: gignomai orqos gignomai an outos dh o nun legw te kai ereunaw ouketi nomos eimi megillos kai kleinia peri o toioutos dunatos eimi nomoqetew prin an kosmew tote de kurios os autos deomai gignomai nomoqetew alla hdh o o toioutos kataskeuazw didaxh meta sunousia polus gignomai an ei gignomai orqos pws tis outos erw fhmi au proteros men dhpou katalekteon an eaw katalogos
english_head: that is what we have now got to discover and state. It is not possible at this stage, Megillus and Clinias, to enact laws for such a body, before it has been duly framed; when it is, its members must themselves ordain what authority they should possess; but it is already plain that what is required in order to form such a body, if it is to be rightly formed, is teaching by means of prolonged conferences. How so? What now are we to understand by this observation? Surely we must first draw up a li

greek_head: τῶν ὅσοι ἐπιτήδειοι πρὸς τὴν τῆς φυλακῆς φύσιν ἂν εἶεν ἡλικίαις τε καὶ μαθημάτων δυνάμεσιν καὶ τρόπων ἤθεσιν καὶ
greek_tokens: o osos epithdeios pros o o fulakeus fus an eien hlikia te kai maqhma dunamis kai tropos hqos kai eqos meta de outos o deomai manqanw oute euriskw radios oute euriskw allos maqhths gignomai pros outos de xronos os te kai en os deomai paralambanw ekastos mataios outos en gramma legw oude gar autos o manqanw
english_head: of all those who are fitted by age, intellectual capacity, and moral character and habit for the office of warden; but as regards the next point, the subjects they should learn,—these it is neither easy to discover for oneself nor is it easy to find another who has made the discovery and learn from him. Moreover, with respect to the limits of time, when and for how long they ought to receive instruction in each subject, it were idle to lay down written regulations;

greek_head: δῆλα γίγνοιτ' ἂν ὅτι πρὸς καιρὸν μανθάνεται πρὶν ἐντὸς τῆς ψυχῆς ἑκάστῳ που μαθήματος ἐπιστήμην γεγονέναι οὕτω
greek_tokens: dhlos gignomai an ostis pros kairos manqanw prin entos o yuxazw ekastos pou maqhma episthmh gignomai outws dh pas o peri outos aporrhtos men legw ou an orqos legw aprorrhtos de dia o mhdeis proerew dhlow o legw tis oun dh poihteos exw outos outws eimi cenos o legw eimi filos en koinos kai mesos eoika egw kew kai eiper kinduneuw peri o politeia eqelw sumpas h tris ec fhmi h trew kubos ballw
english_head: for even the learners themselves could not be sure that they were learning at the opportune time until each of them had acquired within his soul some knowledge of the subject in question. Accordingly, although it would be wrong to term all these matters indescribable, they should be termed imprescribable, seeing that the prescribing of them beforehand does nothing to elucidate the question under discussion. What then must we do, Stranger, under these circumstances? Apparently, my friends, we mus

greek_head: ταῦτα ποιητέον ἐγὼ δ' ὑμῖν συγκινδυνεύσω τῷ φράζειν τε καὶ ἐξηγεῖσθαι τά γε δεδογμένα ἐμοὶ περὶ τῆς παιδείας τε καὶ
greek_tokens: outos poihteos egw de su sugkinduneuw o frazw te kai echgeomai o ge dokew egw peri o paideia te kai trefw o nun au kinew o logos o mentoi kinduneuma ou mikros oude eteros tis prosferhs an eaw sos dh outos ge eimi kleinia melw parakeleuomai su gar o magnhs polis h os an qeos epwnumos autos poihsis kleos aeirw megas kataskeuazw autos orqos h o ge andreios
english_head: and I will go shares with you in the hazard by declaring and explaining my views concerning education and nurture, the subject now started anew in our discourse; but truly the hazard will be no small one, nor comparable to any others. And you, Clinias, I specially exhort to take good heed to this matter. For as concerns the State of the Magnesians—or whoever else, by the god’s direction, gives your State its name,—if you frame it aright, you will achieve most high renown, or at any rate you will

greek_head: εἶναι δοκεῖν τῶν ὕστερον ἐπιγιγνομένων οὐκ ἐκφεύξῃ ποτέ ἐάν γε μὴν οὗτος ἡμῖν ὁ θεῖος γένηται σύλλογος ὦ
greek_tokens: eimi dokew o usteron epigignomai ou ekfeucis pote ean ge meis outos egw o qeios gignomai sullogos eimi filos etairos paradoteos outos o polis amfisbhthsis te ou este oudeis oudeis o nun para outos ws epos eipon nomoqeths ontws de eimi sxedon upar apotelew e mikros prosqen oneiros os o logos efaptw kefalh neomai te koinwnia eikwn tis pws summignumi ean ara egw e te anhr akribhs eklegw paideuw te
english_head: of all your successors. If so be that this divine synod actually comes into existence, my dear colleagues, we must hand over to it the State; and practically all our present lawgivers agree to this without dispute. Thus we shall have as an accomplished fact and waking reality that result which we treated but a short while ago in our discourse as a mere dream, when we constructed a kind of picture of the union of the reason and the head,—if, that is to say, we have the members carefully selected

greek_head: προσηκόντως παιδευθέντες τε ἐν ἀκροπόλει τῆς χώρας κατοικήσαντες φύλακες ἀποτελεσθῶσιν οἵους ἡμεῖς οὐκ εἴδομεν ἐν
greek_tokens: proshkontws paideuw te en akropolis o xwra katoikew fulac apotelew oios egw ou eidon en o prosqen bios pros areth swthria gignomai eimi filos kleinia ek o nun egw erw apas h o polis eateos o katoikisis h o cenos ode ou afeteos alla dehsis kai mhxanh passw koinwnos poihteos epi o o polis katoikisis
english_head: and suitably trained, and after their training quartered in the acropolis of the country, and thus finally made into wardens, the like of whom we have never before seen in our lives for excellence in safeguarding. My dear Clinias, from all that has now been said it follows that either we must forgo the idea of settling the State, or else we must detain this Stranger here, and by prayers and every possible means secure his cooperation in the task of settling the State. That is most true, Megillus

greek_head: Ἀληθέστατα λέγεις ὦ Μέγιλλε καὶ ἐγὼ ποιήσω ταῦθ' οὕτως καὶ σὺ συλλάμβανε
greek_tokens: alhqhs legw eimi megillos kai egw poiew outos outws kai su sullambanw sullambanw
english_head: assist me. Assist you I will.
